topics.title: Determination of TS, TSS and TVS in Water and Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of TS, TSS and TVS in Water and Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Total Solids (TS)** are defined as the residue remaining in a vessel after evaporation of a sample of water or wastewater and subsequent drying in an oven at a defined temperature of **103 °C to 105 °C**. TS represents the sum of all dissolved, suspended, and settleable matter (organic + inorganic) present in the water matrix.

**Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** are defined as the portion of Total Solids retained on a standard glass-fiber filter (nominal pore size **1.5 µm**) after filtration, and subsequently dried to constant weight at **103 °C to 105 °C**. TSS is the operational surrogate for the particulate load in water.

**Total Volatile Solids (TVS)** are defined as the weight loss of the dried residue (from TS or TSS) upon ignition in a muffle furnace at **550 °C ± 50 °C** for a minimum of **1 hour**. TVS is the operational indicator of the **organic matter content**, while the residue that remains after ignition is termed **Fixed Solids (FS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The terminology **"Total Volatile Solids"** is the umbrella term applied to the residue obtained from the TS test. When performed on the residue trapped on the filter, the equivalent parameter is called **"Volatile Suspended Solids (VSS)"** — students must not interchange these names in viva-voce.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tea Strainer" Analogy:** Imagine brewing a strong cup of tea in a muddy stream water.
> - **TS** = Imagine pouring a known cup of this muddy water into a clean empty pan and boiling away all the water. The dry, crusty film left behind (sand, salt, leaves, dust) is your **Total Solids**.
> - **TSS** = Now imagine straining another cup of the same water through a tea strainer (or fine sieve). Whatever gets trapped ON the strainer — the visible specks, mud, leaves — is your **Total Suspended Solids**. The invisible salt and dissolved minerals pass through.
> - **TVS** = If you now burn that strained residue in a hot furnace, the part that burns away (the leaves, organic dust) is your **Volatile Solids**. The sand and ash that doesn't burn is the **Fixed Solids**.

### 1.3 Classification of Solids — Master Overview

$$ \text{Total Solids (TS)} = \text{Total Suspended Solids (TSS)} + \text{Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)} $$

$$ \text{Total Solids (TS)} = \text{Total Volatile Solids (TVS)} + \text{Total Fixed Solids (TFS)} $$

| Sub-Parameter | Symbol | Source Residue | Drying/Ignition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Solids | TS | Whole sample | 103–105 °C |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | Filter-trapped | 103–105 °C |
| Total Dissolved Solids | TDS | Filtrate | 103–105 °C |
| Total Volatile Solids | TVS | TS residue | 550 °C ignition |
| Volatile Suspended Solids | VSS | TSS residue | 550 °C ignition |
| Fixed Solids | FS | TS – TVS | Residual ash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Constants to Memorize:**
> - Drying temperature: **103–105 °C**
> - Ignition temperature: **550 ± 50 °C**
> - Ignition duration: **≥ 1 hour**
> - Filter pore size: **1.5 µm** (glass-fiber)
> - Standard method reference: **APHA, 2540 B / D / E**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The gravimetric determination of solids relies on the principle of **mass conservation under controlled thermal conditions**:

**Step 1 — Evaporation (For TS):**
A measured volume (V) of a well-mixed sample is placed in a pre-weighed evaporating dish (weight = $W_1$). The sample is evaporated to dryness on a steam bath or hot plate, then transferred to a hot air oven at 103–105 °C until a **constant weight** is achieved. The dish is cooled in a desiccator and reweighed ($W_2$).

**Why 103 °C?** This temperature is high enough to drive off mechanically and loosely bound water (including water of crystallization in many salts), but low enough to prevent the volatilization of organic matter and the decomposition of inorganic salts (e.g., bicarbonates, gypsum). Above 105 °C, certain volatile chlorides and organic constituents would be lost, giving underestimates.

**Step 2 — Filtration (For TSS):**
A separate, measured volume of the same well-mixed sample is vacuum-filtered through a pre-weighed glass-fiber filter disc (weight = $W_f$). The filter and retained residue are dried at 103–105 °C, cooled, and weighed ($W_{fr}$).

**Why filtration first?** Suspended solids are operationally defined as the fraction retained on a 1.5 µm filter. This is a convention, not an absolute physical boundary — colloids smaller than 1.5 µm are excluded from TSS but are not truly "dissolved" either.

**Step 3 — Ignition (For TVS):**
The dried residue from Step 1 (or Step 2 for VSS) is placed in a muffle furnace at 550 ± 50 °C for at least 1 hour. The volatile fraction combusts and escapes as CO₂, H₂O vapor, and NOₓ. After cooling in a desiccator, the residue is reweighed ($W_3$). The weight loss = TVS.

**Why 550 °C?** This temperature ensures complete oxidation of organic matter to ash + gases, while retaining the inorganic mineral matrix (silicates, phosphates, oxides of Ca, Mg, Fe, Al). Temperatures above 600 °C can cause volatilization of alkaline chlorides and decomposition of carbonates, leading to overestimation of TVS.

**Step 4 — Constant Weight Verification:**
The drying/ignition cycle is repeated until two successive weighings differ by ≤ **0.5 mg** or by ≤ **4% of the previous weight** (whichever is lower). This ensures all moisture is removed and combustion is complete.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula (SI units) | Variables Explained | Typical Range (mg/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TS** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}$ | $W_1$ = empty dish (mg), $W_2$ = dish + dried residue (mg), $V$ = sample volume (mL) | Potable: < 500, Municipal WW: 700–1500 |
| 2 | **TSS** | $\text{TSS} = \dfrac{(W_{fr} - W_f) \times 1000}{V}$ | $W_f$ = filter weight (mg), $W_{fr}$ = filter + residue (mg), $V$ = sample volume (mL) | Potable: < 100, Municipal WW: 200–500 |
| 3 | **TDS** | $\text{TDS} = \text{TS} - \text{TSS}$ | Computed by difference | Potable: < 500, Municipal WW: 500–1000 |
| 4 | **TVS** | $\text{TVS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_3) \times 1000}{V}$ | $W_2$ = dish + residue (after 105 °C), $W_3$ = dish + ash (after 550 °C) | Municipal WW: 300–600 |
| 5 | **TFS (Fixed Solids)** | $\text{TFS} = \dfrac{(W_3 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}$ | $W_3$ = weight after ignition, $W_1$ = empty dish | Inorganic fraction |
| 6 | **% VSS in TSS** | $\%\,\text{VSS} = \dfrac{\text{VSS}}{\text{TSS}} \times 100$ | Indicator of organic fraction of sludge | Activated sludge: 70–85% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** TS determines whether water is suitable for high-pressure boiler feed (TS < 1 mg/L needed); TSS governs the design of clarifiers, settling tanks, and filtration units in water treatment plants (STPs); TVS/VSS quantifies the biodegradable organic load in activated sludge and helps in calculating the **Food-to-Microorganism (F/M) ratio**.

### 2.3 Reagents, Glassware, and Equipment

- **Glass-fiber filter discs** (Whatman GF/C, 47 mm diameter, 1.5 µm retention) — pre-ashed at 550 °C to remove organic binder and pre-weighed after cooling in a desiccator.
- **Evaporating dishes** (porcelain, 100 mL capacity) — acid-washed, ignited at 550 °C, and stored in a desiccator.
- **Gooch crucible** with asbestos mat (older APHA 2540 D method) — used as an alternative to glass-fiber filters.
- **Drying oven** (gravity convection, thermostatic, capable of 103–105 °C ± 1 °C).
- **Muffle furnace** (capable of 550 °C ± 50 °C).
- **Desiccator** (containing silica gel desiccant) — prevents moisture re-adsorption during cooling.
- **Analytical balance** (readability **0.1 mg**, calibration verified daily).
- **Vacuum filtration assembly** (Buchner funnel + vacuum pump + side-arm flask).
- **Steam bath / hot plate** for initial evaporation.
- **Desiccator tongs** (to avoid thermal shock and contamination).
- **Sample volume pipette / graduated cylinder** (Class A, calibrated).
- **Distilled/deionized water** for blank corrections.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The desiccator is NOT optional. Removing a hot dish into humid laboratory air will cause immediate moisture re-adsorption, producing erratic (and almost always lower) weight readings. This is a 1–2 mark deduction in viva.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Format)

#### A. Pre-treatment of Glassware and Filter (Day 1)

1. **Clean the evaporating dish:** Wash with chromic acid, rinse thoroughly with distilled water, dry in oven at 103–105 °C for 1 hour.
2. **Ignite the dish in muffle furnace at 550 °C for 15 minutes** to remove any residual organic film.
3. **Cool in desiccator for 30 minutes** until it reaches room temperature.
4. **Weigh the empty dish** ($W_1$) on the analytical balance. Record to the nearest **0.1 mg**.
5. **Pre-ash the glass-fiber filter** in the muffle furnace at 550 °C for 15 minutes. Cool in the desiccator and weigh ($W_f$).
6. **Repeat the drying/ignition cycle** for both dish and filter until the difference between two consecutive weighings is ≤ 0.5 mg (constant weight achieved).

#### B. Determination of Total Solids (TS)

1. **Mix the sample thoroughly** by vigorous shaking (homogeneous dispersion of suspended matter is critical).
2. **Pipette a measured volume $V$ (typically 25–100 mL)** into the pre-weighed evaporating dish ($W_1$).
3. **Evaporate to dryness** on a steam bath (preferred to avoid splattering) or a hot plate at low setting.
4. **Transfer the dish to a hot air oven at 103–105 °C** and dry for at least 1 hour.
5. **Cool in the desiccator for 30 minutes** to prevent moisture uptake.
6. **Weigh ($W_2$)** to the nearest 0.1 mg.
7. **Repeat steps 4–6** until the change in weight is ≤ 0.5 mg (constant weight).
8. **Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{(mL)}}}
$$

where the factor **1000** converts mg/mL to mg/L (since 1 L = 1000 mL).

#### C. Determination of Total Suspended Solids (TSS)

1. **Mix the sample** thoroughly as before.
2. **Set up the vacuum filtration assembly** with the pre-weighed glass-fiber filter disc ($W_f$) in the Buchner funnel.
3. **Filter a measured volume $V$ (typically 25–100 mL)** under vacuum. The volume chosen should yield a residue of **2.5–200 mg** on the filter (per APHA guidelines) — too little gives poor precision, too much causes filter clogging and entrained moisture.
4. **Rinse the filter with three successive 10 mL portions of distilled water** to remove dissolved solids trapped in the residue.
5. **Continue suction for 3 minutes** after the final rinse to remove excess water.
6. **Dry the filter in the oven at 103–105 °C for at least 1 hour.**
7. **Cool in the desiccator for 30 minutes.**
8. **Weigh ($W_{fr}$)** to the nearest 0.1 mg.
9. **Repeat until constant weight.**
10. **Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{TSS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_{fr} - W_f) \times 1000}{V_{\text{(mL)}}}
$$

#### D. Determination of Total Volatile Solids (TVS)

1. **Take the dish containing the dried TS residue** (weight $W_2$) from Step B.
2. **Place it in the cold muffle furnace** and heat gradually to **550 °C ± 50 °C** over 30–60 minutes (ramping prevents mechanical loss of light particles due to sudden thermal shock).
3. **Maintain at 550 °C for exactly 1 hour.**
4. **Allow the furnace to cool to ~100 °C** before removing the dish (to prevent thermal shock cracking).
5. **Transfer the dish to the desiccator** and cool to room temperature (~30 min).
6. **Weigh ($W_3$)** to the nearest 0.1 mg.
7. **Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{TVS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_3) \times 1000}{V_{\text{(mL)}}}
$$

The difference $(W_2 - W_3)$ represents the mass of organic matter that combusted and escaped as gas.

#### E. Worked Numerical Example (Sample Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given data for a domestic wastewater sample:**
> - Volume of sample used: $V = 50$ mL
> - Empty evaporating dish weight: $W_1 = 45.6234$ g
> - Dish + residue after drying at 105 °C: $W_2 = 45.6721$ g
> - Dish + residue after ignition at 550 °C: $W_3 = 45.6518$ g
> - Empty filter weight: $W_f = 0.1287$ g
> - Filter + residue after drying at 105 °C: $W_{fr} = 0.1364$ g

**Step 1: Calculate Total Solids (TS)**

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}
$$

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{(45.6721 - 45.6234) \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{0.0487 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{48.7}{50} = 0.974 \text{ g/L} = 974 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Suspended Solids (TSS)**

$$
\text{TSS} = \frac{(W_{fr} - W_f) \times 1000}{V}
$$

$$
\text{TSS} = \frac{(0.1364 - 0.1287) \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TSS} = \frac{0.0077 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TSS} = \frac{7.7}{50} = 0.154 \text{ g/L} = 154 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) by difference**

$$
\text{TDS} = \text{TS} - \text{TSS} = 974 - 154 = 820 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Volatile Solids (TVS)**

$$
\text{TVS} = \frac{(W_2 - W_3) \times 1000}{V}
$$

$$
\text{TVS} = \frac{(45.6721 - 45.6518) \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TVS} = \frac{0.0203 \times 1000}{50}
$$

$$
\text{TVS} = \frac{20.3}{50} = 0.406 \text{ g/L} = 406 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Fixed Solids (TFS)**

$$
\text{TFS} = \text{TS} - \text{TVS} = 974 - 406 = 568 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate % Volatile Fraction (indicator of organic content)**

$$
\%\,\text{TVS} = \left(\frac{\text{TVS}}{\text{TS}}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{406}{974}\right) \times 100 \approx 41.7\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A domestic wastewater with 41.7% volatile fraction indicates a typical municipal sewage signature (range 30–50%). Industrial wastewaters with high inorganic content (e.g., textile, metal finishing) will show % TVS < 20%, while food-processing or dairy effluents may exceed 70%.

#### F. Analytical Matrix — Common Sample Volume Selection (APHA Guidelines)

| Expected TS Range (mg/L) | Recommended Sample Volume (mL) | Expected Residue (mg) |
|---|---|---|
| < 100 | 200–500 | 2.5–50 |
| 100 – 1000 | 100 | 10–100 |
| 1000 – 10,000 | 25–50 | 25–250 |
| 10,000 – 100,000 | 5–10 (with dilution) | 50–500 |
| > 100,000 | Dilute and re-analyze | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect well-mixed water/wastewater sample] --> B[Sample Preparation: Mix thoroughly and measure volume V]
    B --> C1[TS Test Branch]
    B --> C2[TSS Test Branch]
    B --> C3[TVS Test Branch]

    C1 --> D1[Step 1: Pre-weigh empty evaporating dish W1]
    D1 --> D2[Step 2: Transfer V mL sample to dish]
    D2 --> D3[Step 3: Evaporate to dryness on steam bath]
    D3 --> D4[Step 4: Dry in oven at 103 to 105 C for 1 hour]
    D4 --> D5[Step 5: Cool in desiccator for 30 min]
    D5 --> D6[Step 6: Weigh W2 to 0.1 mg]
    D6 --> D7{Constant weight achieved?}
    D7 -->|No| D4
    D7 -->|Yes| D8[Compute TS = W2 minus W1 times 1000 divided by V]

    C2 --> E1[Step 1: Pre-weigh glass fiber filter Wf]
    E1 --> E2[Step 2: Set up vacuum filtration assembly]
    E2 --> E3[Step 3: Filter V mL sample through 1.5 micron filter]
    E3 --> E4[Step 4: Rinse filter with 3 x 10 mL distilled water]
    E4 --> E5[Step 5: Dry in oven at 103 to 105 C for 1 hour]
    E5 --> E6[Step 6: Cool in desiccator for 30 min]
    E6 --> E7[Step 7: Weigh Wfr to 0.1 mg]
    E7 --> E8{Constant weight achieved?}
    E8 -->|No| E5
    E8 -->|Yes| E9[Compute TSS = Wfr minus Wf times 1000 divided by V]

    C3 --> F1[Step 1: Use dried TS residue of weight W2]
    F1 --> F2[Step 2: Place dish in cold muffle furnace]
    F2 --> F3[Step 3: Ramp temperature to 550 C over 30 to 60 min]
    F3 --> F4[Step 4: Hold at 550 C for 1 hour]
    F4 --> F5[Step 5: Cool furnace to 100 C, transfer dish to desiccator]
    F5 --> F6[Step 6: Weigh W3 to 0.1 mg]
    F6 --> F7[Compute TVS = W2 minus W3 times 1000 divided by V]
    F7 --> F8[Compute TFS = TS minus TVS]

    D8 --> G[Final Output: Report TS, TDS, TSS, TVS, TFS in mg/L]
    E9 --> G
    F8 --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Solids Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph raw[Raw Water or Wastewater Sample]
        direction TB
        R[Total Sample Volume V]
    end

    R -->|Evaporate at 103 to 105 C| TS_block[Total Solids TS]
    TS_block -->|Ignite at 550 C| TVS_block[Total Volatile Solids TVS]
    TS_block -->|Residue after ignition| TFS_block[Total Fixed Solids TFS]

    R -->|Filter through 1.5 micron| TSS_block[Total Suspended Solids TSS]
    R -->|Filtrate evaporates| TDS_block[Total Dissolved Solids TDS]

    TSS_block -->|Ignite at 550 C| VSS_block[Volatile Suspended Solids VSS]
    TSS_block -->|Residue after ignition| FSS_block[Fixed Suspended Solids FSS]

    TDS_block -->|Ignite at 550 C| VDS_block[Volatile Dissolved Solids VDS]
    TDS_block -->|Residue after ignition| FDS_block[Fixed Dissolved Solids FDS]

    TS_block -.equals.->|TSS + TDS| TSS_block
    TS_block -.equals.->|TVS + TFS| TVS_block
```

### 4.3 Vacuum Filtration Assembly — Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Buchner Funnel with pre-weighed GF/C filter] --> B[Vacuum Flask or Side-arm flask]
    B --> C[Vacuum Pump with pressure regulator]
    A -.suction holds filter.-> B
    B -.measured filtrate collected.-> D[Discard or analyze for TDS]
    A --> E[Apply sample V mL and apply vacuum]
    E --> F[Rinse with 3 x 10 mL distilled water]
    F --> G[Dry filter at 103 to 105 C]
    G --> H[Cool in desiccator]
    H --> I[Weigh Wfr]
```

### 4.4 Sources of Error — Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ERR[Sources of Error in Solids Determination]
    ERR --> S1[Sampling]
    ERR --> S2[Equipment]
    ERR --> S3[Procedure]
    ERR --> S4[Environment]
    ERR --> S5[Operator]

    S1 --> I1[Non-representative sample]
    S1 --> I2[Insufficient mixing]
    S1 --> I3[Sample degradation during storage]

    S2 --> I4[Uncalibrated balance]
    S2 --> I5[Inaccurate oven temperature]
    S2 --> I6[Contaminated glassware]
    S2 --> I7[Wet desiccant]

    S3 --> I8[Insufficient drying time]
    S3 --> I9[Filter clogging]
    S3 --> I10[Thermal shock to dish]
    S3 --> I11[Incomplete ignition]

    S4 --> I12[High humidity lab air]
    S4 --> I13[Airborne dust deposition]
    S4 --> I14[Vibration affecting balance]

    S5 --> I15[Improper weighing technique]
    S5 --> I16[Hot dish handling]
    S5 --> I17[Forgetting blank correction]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with one example each: (a) Total Solids (TS), (b) Total Suspended Solids (TSS), (c) Total Volatile Solids (TVS).**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Total Solids (TS):** TS is the residue remaining after evaporating a known volume of water/wastewater to dryness and drying it in a hot air oven at 103–105 °C to constant weight. It represents the sum of all dissolved, suspended, and settleable matter in the sample.
*Example: For a municipal wastewater, TS is typically 700–1500 mg/L.*

**(b) Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** TSS is that portion of TS that is retained on a 1.5 µm glass-fiber filter after filtration of a known volume of sample, and subsequently dried to constant weight at 103–105 °C. It is a measure of the particulate (non-dissolved) load.
*Example: Secondary clarifier effluent should have TSS < 30 mg/L for treated wastewater discharge.*

**(c) Total Volatile Solids (TVS):** TVS is the loss in weight of the dried TS residue upon ignition in a muffle furnace at 550 ± 50 °C for at least 1 hour. It is an operational indicator of organic matter content.
*Example: In an activated sludge process, VSS of mixed liquor is typically 2000–4000 mg/L.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the drying temperature fixed at 103–105 °C for TS/TSS determination, and the ignition temperature fixed at 550 °C for TVS? What happens if these limits are exceeded?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Drying at 103–105 °C:** This temperature range is sufficient to drive off mechanically bound and adsorbed water, including most water of crystallization, yet low enough to prevent the loss of volatile organic matter and decomposition of inorganic salts like bicarbonates, gypsum, and most chlorides. It is the internationally accepted compromise in APHA Standard Methods 2540 B and D.

- **Ignition at 550 °C:** This temperature is hot enough to ensure complete oxidation of all organic carbon to CO₂ and water vapor, but cool enough to retain the inorganic mineral matrix (silicates, oxides of Ca, Mg, Fe, Al, and phosphates).

- **Consequences of exceeding limits:**
  - If drying temperature > 105 °C: organic matter volatilizes and some inorganic salts (chlorides, nitrates) decompose → TS/TSS are **underestimated**.
  - If ignition temperature > 600 °C: alkaline chlorides volatilize, carbonates decompose to CO₂ → TVS is **overestimated**, and ash weight ($W_3$) decreases falsely.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — Option 1 (Internal Choice)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the experimental procedure for the determination of Total Solids (TS) in a wastewater sample. List the apparatus, reagents, and precautions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- Pre-weighed evaporating dish (porcelain, ignited at 550 °C)
- Hot air oven (103–105 °C)
- Muffle furnace
- Analytical balance (0.1 mg readability)
- Desiccator with silica gel
- Steam bath / hot plate
- Pipette / graduated cylinder (Class A)
- Tongs (desiccator-type)

**Reagents:** Distilled / deionized water for rinsing (no chemical reagents required for the TS test itself).

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Pre-treatment:** Clean the evaporating dish, ignite in muffle furnace at 550 °C for 15 min, cool in desiccator, and weigh to constant weight ($W_1$).
2. **Sample evaporation:** Pipette a known volume ($V$ mL, typically 25–100 mL) of well-mixed sample into the dish. Evaporate to dryness on a steam bath.
3. **Drying:** Transfer the dish to a hot air oven at 103–105 °C for at least 1 hour.
4. **Cooling and weighing:** Cool in desiccator for 30 min, weigh to 0.1 mg ($W_2$).
5. **Constant weight:** Repeat drying-cooling-weighing cycle until successive weighings differ by ≤ 0.5 mg.
6. **Calculation:**

$$
\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}
$$

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Sample must be well-mixed immediately before withdrawal.
- The dish must be cooled in the desiccator to prevent moisture re-adsorption.
- Avoid splattering during evaporation (use steam bath).
- The volume of sample should yield a residue between 2.5 mg and 200 mg.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**A 50 mL wastewater sample was analyzed for TS, TSS, and TVS. The following data were recorded:**
- Empty dish: $W_1$ = 47.5826 g
- Dish + dried residue: $W_2$ = 47.6311 g
- Dish + residue after ignition: $W_3$ = 47.6098 g
- Empty filter: $W_f$ = 0.1421 g
- Filter + dried residue: $W_{fr}$ = 0.1496 g

**Calculate TS, TSS, TDS, TVS, and TFS in mg/L, and comment on the organic strength of the wastewater. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Total Solids (TS)** (1.5 marks)

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{(47.6311 - 47.5826) \times 1000}{50} = \frac{0.0485 \times 1000}{50} = 970 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 2: Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** (1.5 marks)

$$
\text{TSS} = \frac{(0.1496 - 0.1421) \times 1000}{50} = \frac{0.0075 \times 1000}{50} = 150 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3: Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** (1 mark)

$$
\text{TDS} = \text{TS} - \text{TSS} = 970 - 150 = 820 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 4: Total Volatile Solids (TVS)** (1.5 marks)

$$
\text{TVS} = \frac{(47.6311 - 47.6098) \times 1000}{50} = \frac{0.0213 \times 1000}{50} = 426 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 5: Total Fixed Solids (TFS)** (0.5 marks)

$$
\text{TFS} = \text{TS} - \text{TVS} = 970 - 426 = 544 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 6: Comment on organic strength** (1 mark)

$$
\%\,\text{TVS} = \left(\frac{426}{970}\right) \times 100 \approx 43.9\%
$$

The volatile fraction is ~44%, which lies within the typical range for domestic municipal wastewater (30–50%). The wastewater exhibits **moderate-to-high organic strength**, indicating effective biological treatment will be required before disposal. The inorganic load (TFS = 544 mg/L) is also significant, suggesting possible mixing with industrial effluents or high mineral content in the source water.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Describe the procedure for the determination of Total Volatile Solids (TVS) in a wastewater sample. Why is a desiccator used at every weighing step? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Obtain the dried TS residue** in the evaporating dish (mass $W_2$, recorded from the TS test).
2. **Place the dish in a cold muffle furnace** and gradually raise the temperature to 550 ± 50 °C over a period of 30–60 minutes (slow ramping prevents sudden combustion and loss of ash).
3. **Maintain at 550 °C for exactly 1 hour** to ensure complete combustion of organic matter.
4. **Allow the furnace to cool** to about 100 °C (to avoid thermal shock).
5. **Transfer the dish to the desiccator** and cool to room temperature (~30 min).
6. **Weigh the dish to 0.1 mg** ($W_3$).
7. **Calculation:**

$$
\text{TVS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_3) \times 1000}{V}
$$

8. The residue that remains (Fixed Solids) is also reported as:

$$
\text{TFS (mg/L)} = \text{TS} - \text{TVS}
$$

**Why the desiccator? (2 marks):**

- A desiccator contains a **drying agent (silica gel / anhydrous CaCl₂)** that maintains a moisture-free microenvironment.
- A dish taken directly from a hot oven into open laboratory air will rapidly re-adsorb moisture from the humid atmosphere, causing **erratic and falsely elevated weight readings**.
- Cooling the dish to ambient temperature in the desiccator ensures a **stable, reproducible weighing condition** and allows the dish to reach thermal equilibrium with the balance.
- The desiccator must be regularly checked and the desiccant must be **regenerated (dried in oven at 105 °C)** when it turns pink (indicating saturation with moisture).

**Precautions:**
- Do not ignite a hot dish (introduce it cold and ramp up).
- Use crucible tongs; never handle with bare hands.
- Run a reagent blank to account for any loss of dish material during ignition.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Total Solids (TS), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Total Volatile Solids (TVS), and Volatile Suspended Solids (VSS) with respect to (i) the operational procedure, (ii) the residue tested, and (iii) typical significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Procedure (2 marks) | Residue Tested (2 marks) | Significance (3 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TS** | Evaporate whole sample, dry at 103–105 °C | Whole sample (all fractions) | Total pollution load; suitability for boiler feed, cooling water |
| **TSS** | Filter sample through 1.5 µm filter, dry filter + residue at 103–105 °C | Particulates retained on filter | Particulate pollution; design of clarifiers & filters; effluent quality (BOD/TSS ratio check) |
| **TDS** | Evaporate the filtrate from TSS test at 103–105 °C, or compute TS – TSS | Dissolved salts & minerals | Salinity; corrosion potential; drinking water palatability; irrigation suitability (SAR) |
| **TVS** | Ignite dried TS residue at 550 °C | Whole sample (organic fraction of TS) | Total organic load; helps in sludge digestion calculations |
| **VSS** | Ignite dried TSS residue at 550 °C | Particulates retained on filter (organic fraction) | Active biomass in activated sludge; F/M ratio calculation; sludge age (θc) |

**Key Distinguishing Features:**

1. TS and TVS are determined on the **whole sample** (no filtration).
2. TSS and VSS are determined on the **filtered residue**.
3. TDS is the **complement** of TSS within TS.
4. TVS and VSS are **operational surrogates for organic content** (not absolute, since some inorganics like CaCO₃ can decompose at 550 °C).
5. In STP design, **VSS/TSS ratio indicates sludge "activity"**; healthy activated sludge has VSS/TSS ≈ 0.70–0.85.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Reporting TS as 0.974 g/L instead of **974 mg/L** is a frequent error. Always convert g/L to mg/L by multiplying by 1000.
> 2. **Forgetting the Desiccator Step (–1 to –2 marks):** A dish cooled in open air is not a valid weighing — it introduces uncontrolled moisture.
> 3. **Confusing TVS with VSS (–1 mark):** TVS uses the **TS residue**; VSS uses the **TSS residue (filter)**. They are different parameters.
> 4. **Not Achieving Constant Weight (–1 mark):** A single drying cycle is insufficient; the procedure must be repeated until successive weighings differ by ≤ 0.5 mg.
> 5. **Sample Volume Out of Range (–1 mark):** Using a volume that yields < 2.5 mg or > 200 mg residue gives poor accuracy. The APHA-prescribed range is 2.5–200 mg.
> 6. **Wrong Filter (–1 mark):** Using ordinary filter paper (Whatman No. 42) instead of glass-fiber filter (Whatman GF/C, 1.5 µm) is a procedural deviation.
> 7. **Hot Dish Handling (–1 mark):** Direct hand contact transfers oils and moisture; always use desiccator tongs.
> 8. **No Reagent Blank (–1 mark):** Failure to subtract the blank weight loss of the filter (when GF/C is pre-ashed) or dish.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **TS** = residue after evaporating + drying whole sample at **103–105 °C**.
> - **TSS** = residue on **1.5 µm glass-fiber filter** after drying at 103–105 °C.
> - **TDS** = TS − TSS (computed by difference, or by evaporating filtrate).
> - **TVS** = weight loss of **TS residue** upon ignition at **550 ± 50 °C for ≥ 1 hour**.
> - **VSS** = weight loss of **TSS residue** (filter + trapped solids) at 550 °C.
> - **TFS / FSS** = the ash that remains after ignition = inorganic fraction.
> - **Universal formula (gravimetric basis):** $\text{Parameter (mg/L)} = \dfrac{\Delta W \text{ (mg)} \times 1000}{V \text{ (mL)}}$
> - **Constant weight criterion:** Δm ≤ 0.5 mg between two consecutive weighings.
> - **Cooling:** Always in a **desiccator** containing active silica gel.
> - **Weighing precision:** Analytical balance with readability **0.1 mg**.
> - **Sample volume selection:** Adjust $V$ so that residue lies in **2.5–200 mg** range.
> - **Filter:** Glass-fiber (Whatman GF/C, 47 mm, 1.5 µm) — pre-ashed at 550 °C.
> - **Significance in STP design:** TSS governs clarifier design; TVS/VSS governs biological treatment kinetics and F/M ratio.
> - **APHA Reference:** Standard Methods 2540 B (TS), 2540 D (TSS), 2540 E (TVS).
> - **Typical municipal WW ranges:** TS = 700–1500 mg/L; TSS = 200–500 mg/L; TVS = 300–600 mg/L.
> - **% TVS of healthy activated sludge VSS/TSS = 70–85%** — a key design rule of thumb.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Alkalinity and Acidity of Water and Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Alkalinity and Acidity of Water and Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Alkalinity — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Alkalinity** is the quantitative capacity of a water sample to neutralize strong acids to a designated pH endpoint, expressed conventionally in milligrams per litre (mg/L) as equivalent calcium carbonate (CaCO₃).

In precise KTU 2024 terminology, alkalinity represents the **sum of all titratable bases** present in the water matrix. It is fundamentally produced by the presence of three primary species:

1. **Hydroxides (OH⁻)** — e.g., from NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂ contamination
2. **Carbonates (CO₃²⁻)** — e.g., from Na₂CO₃, CaCO₃ dissolution
3. **Bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻)** — e.g., from Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂

Minor contributions may also arise from **borates, silicates, phosphates, and organic acid salts**, but in routine environmental engineering practice, the carbonate system dominates.

## 1.2 Acidity — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acidity** is the quantitative capacity of a water sample to neutralize strong bases to a designated pH endpoint, expressed in mg/L as equivalent CaCO₃.

Acidity is generated by:
- **Mineral acids** (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) — from industrial discharge or acid rain
- **Dissolved carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — from atmospheric dissolution or biological respiration
- **Organic acids** (humic, fulvic, tannic) — from decaying vegetation
- **Salts of strong acids and weak bases** (e.g., FeCl₃, Al₂(SO₄)₃) — from mine drainage

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Buffer Tank" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a swimming pool's pH as a tightrope walker's balance pole.
> - **Alkalinity** is the **weight of the pole** — the heavier it is, the harder the wind (acid rain, pollutants) has to push before the walker falls.
> - **Acidity** is the **storm pushing the walker** — strong winds (acids) try to knock the system over.

In the laboratory, we add measured amounts of **opposing chemical "wind"** to determine the strength of these systems. We never measure alkalinity/acidity directly with a single number; we measure **how much standard acid or base the sample can consume** before reaching a fixed pH.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ | **50 g/equivalent** |
| Standard titrant (Alkalinity) | **0.02 N H₂SO₄** or HCl |
| Standard titrant (Acidity) | **0.02 N NaOH** |
| Phenolphthalein endpoint pH | **8.3** |
| Methyl orange endpoint pH | **4.5** |
| Sample volume (standard) | **50 mL or 100 mL** |
| Reporting unit | **mg/L as CaCO₃** |
| Drinking water limit (IS 10500) | **200 mg/L (TDS)** / **No specific for Alkalinity** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Alkalinity Titration

The alkalinity titration is a **two-stage acid-base neutralization** procedure, exploiting the differential acid demand of carbonate species at two distinct pH endpoints.

### 2.1.1 Stage 1 — Phenolphthalein Endpoint (P)

At pH **8.3**, the equivalence point corresponds to the **complete conversion of hydroxide and carbonate species to bicarbonate**:

$$\text{OH}^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{HCO}_3^-$$

**Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (P)** titrates: **all OH⁻ + ½ of CO₃²⁻**

### 2.1.2 Stage 2 — Methyl Orange / Total Endpoint (T)

At pH **4.5**, the equivalence point corresponds to the **complete neutralization of bicarbonate to carbonic acid**:

$$\text{HCO}_3^- + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$

**Total Alkalinity (T)** titrates: **all OH⁻ + all CO₃²⁻ + all HCO₃⁻**

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of Acidity Titration

Acidity titration is the **mirror image** of alkalinity titration, using a strong base (NaOH) as titrant.

- **Phenolphthalein Acidity (P)** — measures strong mineral acids + weak acids titrated up to pH 8.3
- **Methyl Orange Acidity (T)** — measures total acidity, including weak acids and CO₂ converted to HCO₃⁻

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU board question on this topic.**

### Primary Alkalinity / Acidity Formula

$$\boxed{\text{Alkalinity (or Acidity) mg/L as CaCO}_3 = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Volume of titrant used (mL)
- $N$ = Normality of titrant (eq/L)
- $50{,}000$ = $\dfrac{50 \text{ (Eq. wt. of CaCO}_3\text{)} \times 1000 \text{ (mg/g)}}{\text{conversion factor}}$
- $V_s$ = Volume of sample taken (mL)

### Alkalinity Species Relationship Table

| Condition | OH⁻ (mg/L) | CO₃²⁻ (mg/L) | HCO₃⁻ (mg/L) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| P = 0 | 0 | 0 | T |
| P < T/2 | 0 | 2P | T − 2P |
| P = T/2 | 0 | 2P (= T) | 0 |
| P > T/2 | 2P − T | 2(T − P) | 0 |
| P = T | T | 0 | 0 |

**Where:**
- P = Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃)
- T = Total Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃)

## 2.4 Detailed Procedure for Alkalinity Determination

### Apparatus Required
1. **Burette** (50 mL, least count 0.05 mL)
2. **Pipette** (50 mL or 100 mL, Class A)
3. **Conical flask (Erlenmeyer)** — 250 mL
4. **Measuring cylinder** — 100 mL
5. **White porcelain tile** (for endpoint visualization)

### Reagents Required
1. **Standard Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄), 0.02 N** — primary titrant
2. **Phenolphthalein indicator** (1% in 90% ethanol)
3. **Methyl orange indicator** (0.05% aqueous)

### Procedure Steps

**Step 1 — Sample Collection:**
Rinse the sample bottle 2–3 times with the water to be tested. Collect 200–500 mL of sample. For wastewater, preserve by cooling to 4 °C and analyze within 24 hours.

**Step 2 — Pipetting:**
Pipette **50 mL (or 100 mL)** of the well-mixed sample into a clean 250 mL conical flask.

**Step 3 — Phenolphthalein Endpoint (P):**
Add **2–3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator**. If the solution turns **pink**, titrate against standard 0.02 N H₂SO₄ from the burette until the **pink colour just disappears**. Note this volume as $V_p$.

If no pink colour develops initially → **P = 0** (no hydroxide or carbonate alkalinity present).

**Step 4 — Methyl Orange Endpoint (T):**
To the same flask (now colourless), add **2–3 drops of methyl orange indicator**. The solution turns **yellow**. Continue titration with the same H₂SO₄ until the colour changes from yellow → **orange/salmon pink** (NOT red).

If the original sample had no pink colour (P = 0), start directly with methyl orange. Note this total burette reading as $V_t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Many students add methyl orange to a *fresh* sample instead of continuing the same flask. This is acceptable for *Total* alkalinity, but for species analysis, the *same flask* must be used.

## 2.5 Detailed Procedure for Acidity Determination

### Reagents
1. **Standard Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), 0.02 N** — primary titrant
2. **Phenolphthalein indicator**
3. **Methyl orange indicator**

### Procedure Steps

**Step 1:** Pipette **50 mL or 100 mL** of sample into a 250 mL conical flask.

**Step 2 — Phenolphthalein Acidity:**
Add 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein. If the solution is **colourless**, titrate with 0.02 N NaOH until a faint **pink** colour persists for 30 seconds. Note the volume as $V_p$.

If pink colour appears immediately → **no mineral acid acidity** (P = 0).

**Step 3 — Methyl Orange Acidity (Total):**
To the same flask, add 2–3 drops of methyl orange. The solution turns **red** (acidic). Continue titration with NaOH until it turns **yellow/orange** (just before yellow). Note total volume as $V_t$.

For samples with high CO₂, **boil the sample for 2 minutes** after the methyl orange endpoint, cool, and re-titrate to expel dissolved CO₂.

## 2.6 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this experiment critical in real-world environmental engineering?**

1. **Drinking water treatment** — Excess alkalinity causes scaling in pipes; low alkalinity leads to corrosive water that leaches metals (Pb, Cu) from plumbing.
2. **Boiler feed water** — Alkalinity must be minimized to prevent scale formation and boiler carryover.
3. **Wastewater treatment** — Acidity indicates industrial contamination (e.g., acid mine drainage); alkalinity indicates domestic sewage strength.
4. **Aquatic ecosystem health** — Alkalinity buffers lakes/rivers against pH changes from acid rain.
5. **Coagulation chemistry** — Alum dosing in water treatment depends on raw water alkalinity to maintain pH.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Alkalinity Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

Alkalinity is a measure of the **equivalent concentration of bases** in water. We express it as CaCO₃ because CaCO₃ has a convenient equivalent weight.

**Equivalent weight of CaCO₃:**
$$\text{E.W. of CaCO}_3 = \frac{\text{Molecular Weight}}{n} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

(where $n = 2$ because CaCO₃ yields 2 OH⁻ equivalents in reaction with acid)

### 3.1.2 Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Equivalents of acid used:**
$$\text{Equivalents of acid} = N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \text{ (in L)}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalents of base in sample (by neutralization law):**
$$\text{Equivalents of base} = N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \text{ (L)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to grams of CaCO₃ equivalent:**
$$\text{Mass of CaCO}_3 = N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \text{ (L)} \times 50 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg:**
$$\text{Mass of CaCO}_3 = N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \text{ (L)} \times 50 \times 1000 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 5 — Express per litre of sample:**
$$\text{Alkalinity (mg/L)} = \frac{N_{acid} \times V_{acid} \text{ (L)} \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample} \text{ (L)}}$$

**Step 6 — Express $V_{acid}$ in mL (so $V_{sample}$ must also be in mL):**

$$\boxed{\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}}$$

Where $V$ and $V_s$ are **both in mL**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Basic Alkalinity Calculation

**Problem:** A 100 mL water sample required 8.5 mL of 0.02 N H₂SO₄ to reach the methyl orange endpoint. Calculate the total alkalinity in mg/L as CaCO₃.

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.
- $V = 8.5$ mL
- $N = 0.02$ N
- $V_s = 100$ mL

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.
$$\text{Alkalinity} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.
$$\text{Alkalinity} = \frac{8.5 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator.
$$8.5 \times 0.02 = 0.17$$
$$0.17 \times 50{,}000 = 8500$$

**Step 5:** Divide by sample volume.
$$\text{Alkalinity} = \frac{8500}{100} = 85 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Alkalinity Species Distribution

**Problem:** A 50 mL water sample required 4.2 mL of 0.02 N H₂SO₄ to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. A further 7.8 mL was needed to reach the methyl orange endpoint. Calculate P, T, and the species distribution.

**Step 1:** Calculate P (Phenolphthalein Alkalinity).
$$P = \frac{4.2 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{50} = \frac{4200}{50} = 84 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate T (Total Alkalinity).
$$T = \frac{(4.2 + 7.8) \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{50} = \frac{12{,}000}{50} = 240 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** Determine the relationship.
$$T/2 = 240/2 = 120 \text{ mg/L}$$
$$P = 84 \text{ mg/L} < 120 \text{ mg/L} = T/2$$

Since $P < T/2$, we use the second row of the relationship table:
- OH⁻ = **0 mg/L**
- CO₃²⁻ = $2P = 2 \times 84 = \mathbf{168}$ **mg/L as CaCO₃**
- HCO₃⁻ = $T - 2P = 240 - 168 = \mathbf{72}$ **mg/L as CaCO₃**

**Step 4:** Verification.
$$2P + (T - 2P) = 168 + 72 = 240 = T \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Acidity Calculation

**Problem:** A 100 mL industrial wastewater sample required 12.5 mL of 0.1 N NaOH to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. Calculate the phenolphthalein acidity in mg/L as CaCO₃.

**Step 1:** Identify values.
- $V = 12.5$ mL
- $N = 0.1$ N
- $V_s = 100$ mL

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.
$$\text{Acidity} = \frac{12.5 \times 0.1 \times 50{,}000}{100} = \frac{62{,}500}{100} = 625 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Interpretation:** This high value (> 500 mg/L) indicates **strong industrial acidic contamination** requiring neutralization treatment before disposal.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Common Error Zones

| Step | Correct Approach | Common Student Error | Marks Lost |
|------|------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Indicator addition | 2–3 drops only | 5–6 drops, dilute endpoint | 1–2 |
| Endpoint reading | Just-disappeared pink | Over-titrated to clear | 1–2 |
| Same flask usage | Continue titration in same flask | Use fresh sample | 2–3 |
| Unit conversion | $V$ in mL with $V_s$ in mL | Mixing mL and L | 2 |
| Species selection | Use correct row of table | Wrong conditional branch | 3 |
| Blank correction | Subtract blank if any | Skip blank titration | 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Alkalinity Titration — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Water Sample 50-100 mL] --> pipetteA[Pipette into 250 mL Conical Flask]
    pipetteA --> addPhA[Add 2-3 drops Phenolphthalein Indicator]
    addPhA --> checkA{Is solution PINK?}
    checkA -- Yes --> titrateA1[Titrate with 0.02 N H2SO4 until pink JUST disappears]
    checkA -- No --> recordPA0[Record P = 0]
    titrateA1 --> recordPA[Record Volume Vp as Phenolphthalein Alkalinity]
    recordPA0 --> addMOA
    recordPA --> addMOA[Add 2-3 drops Methyl Orange to SAME flask]
    addMOA --> colorYellow[Solution turns YELLOW]
    colorYellow --> titrateA2[Continue titration until YELLOW becomes ORANGE]
    titrateA2 --> recordTA[Record Total Volume Vt from burette]
    recordTA --> calcP[Calculate P mg/L using formula]
    recordTA --> calcT[Calculate T mg/L using formula]
    calcP --> compare{P vs T/2?}
    calcT --> compare
    compare -- P equals 0 --> sp1[Only HCO3- = T]
    compare -- P less than T/2 --> sp2[OH equals 0, CO3 = 2P, HCO3 = T minus 2P]
    compare -- P equals T/2 --> sp3[CO3 = 2P equals T, OH equals 0, HCO3 equals 0]
    compare -- P greater than T/2 --> sp4[OH = 2P minus T, CO3 = 2T minus 2P, HCO3 equals 0]
    compare -- P equals T --> sp5[Only OH = T, no CO3 or HCO3]
    sp1 --> resultA[Final Report: P, T, OH, CO3, HCO3 in mg/L as CaCO3]
    sp2 --> resultA
    sp3 --> resultA
    sp4 --> resultA
    sp5 --> resultA
```

## 4.2 Acidity Titration — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Start: Wastewater Sample 50-100 mL] --> pipetteB[Pipette into 250 mL Conical Flask]
    pipetteB --> addPhB[Add 2-3 drops Phenolphthalein Indicator]
    addPhB --> checkB{Is solution COLOURLESS?}
    checkB -- Yes --> titrateB1[Titrate with 0.02 N NaOH until faint PINK appears]
    checkB -- No --> recordPB0[Record Phenolphthalein Acidity P = 0]
    titrateB1 --> recordPB[Record Volume Vp]
    recordPB0 --> addMOB
    recordPB --> addMOB[Add 2-3 drops Methyl Orange to SAME flask]
    addMOB --> colorRed[Solution turns RED acidic]
    colorRed --> titrateB2[Continue titration with 0.02 N NaOH]
    titrateB2 --> checkCO2{High CO2 suspected?}
    checkCO2 -- Yes --> boilB[Boil 2 minutes, cool, re-titrate]
    checkCO2 -- No --> titrateB3
    boilB --> titrateB3[Continue until RED turns YELLOW-ORANGE]
    titrateB3 --> recordTB[Record Total Volume Vt]
    recordTB --> calcPB[Calculate Phenolphthalein Acidity mg/L]
    recordTB --> calcTB[Calculate Total Acidity mg/L]
    calcPB --> resultB[Final Report: P and T in mg/L as CaCO3]
    calcTB --> resultB
```

## 4.3 Carbonate Species Distribution Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sample[Water Sample] --> titrate1[Titrate to pH 8.3]
    titrate1 --> volP[Volume P captured]
    sample --> titrate2[Titrate to pH 4.5]
    titrate2 --> volT[Volume T captured]
    volP --> compareLogic{Check P versus T relationship}
    volT --> compareLogic
    compareLogic --> case1[Case 1: P equals 0 - Bicarbonate only]
    compareLogic --> case2[Case 2: P less than T/2 - Carbonate and Bicarbonate]
    compareLogic --> case3[Case 3: P equals T/2 - Pure Carbonate]
    compareLogic --> case4[Case 4: P greater than T/2 - Hydroxide and Carbonate]
    compareLogic --> case5[Case 5: P equals T - Pure Hydroxide]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Burette Reading Schematic (Text-Based Schematic)

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │            BURETTE (50 mL)             │
   │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐  │
   │  │  0  ●─────────────────────  50   │  │
   │  │  5  │                        45  │  │
   │  │  10 │   ← Meniscus (eye level) 40 │  │
   │  │  15  │                          35 │  │
   │  │  20  │         ▼                30 │  │
   │  │  25  │   (concave meniscus)     25 │  │
   │  │  30  │                          20 │  │
   │  │  35  │                          15 │  │
   │  │  40  │                          10 │  │
   │  │  45  │                           5 │  │
   │  │  50  │                           0 │  │
   │  └──────────────────────────────────┘  │
   │                                        │
   │  Initial reading: 0.00 mL              │
   │  Final reading:   8.50 mL              │
   │  Volume used:     8.50 mL              │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  │
                  │  drips into ↓
                  ▼
   ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  CONICAL FLASK (250 mL)            │
   │    ● 50 mL sample                  │
   │    ● 2 drops Phenolphthalein       │
   │    ● White tile underneath         │
   │    ● Swirl continuously            │
   └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define alkalinity. What are the causes of alkalinity in natural water?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Alkalinity** is the capacity of water to neutralize acids, expressed in mg/L as CaCO₃. (1 mark)

**Causes of alkalinity:**
1. **Bicarbonates** — Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂ from weathering of carbonate rocks (1 mark)
2. **Carbonates** — CaCO₃, MgCO₃ from limestone contact (0.5 marks)
3. **Hydroxides** — from industrial/domestic wastewater contamination (0.5 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the indicators used in alkalinity titration along with their endpoint pH values.**

**Model Answer:**

| Indicator | Endpoint pH | Species Neutralized |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Phenolphthalein | **8.3** | OH⁻ + ½ CO₃²⁻ → HCO₃⁻ |
| Methyl Orange | **4.5** | Complete neutralization including HCO₃⁻ → H₂CO₃ |

(3 marks — 1.5 marks each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the procedure for the determination of alkalinity of a water sample with a neat sketch. Indicate the role of indicators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (1 mark):**
Alkalinity is determined by titrating the water sample with a standard strong acid (0.02 N H₂SO₄) to two pH endpoints using two indicators: phenolphthalein (pH 8.3) and methyl orange (pH 4.5).

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Sample preparation:** Pipette 50 mL (or 100 mL) of well-mixed water sample into a 250 mL clean conical flask.
2. **Phenolphthalein titration:** Add 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator. If pink colour develops, titrate against 0.02 N H₂SO₄ from the burette with constant swirling until the pink colour just disappears. Note the burette reading as $V_p$.
3. **Methyl orange titration:** To the same flask, add 2–3 drops of methyl orange. The solution turns yellow. Continue titration with 0.02 N H₂SO₄ until the colour changes from yellow to a faint orange/salmon pink (NOT red). Note the total burette reading as $V_t$.
4. **Record** the values and calculate using:
$$\text{Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$$

**Role of indicators (2 marks):**
- **Phenolphthalein (pH 8.3):** Detects the equivalence point when all OH⁻ and ½ of CO₃²⁻ are neutralized to HCO₃⁻.
- **Methyl orange (pH 4.5):** Detects the complete neutralization of all alkaline species including HCO₃⁻ → H₂CO₃.

**(b)** 100 mL of a water sample required 6.5 mL of 0.02 N H₂SO₄ to phenolphthalein endpoint. A further 13.5 mL was required for the methyl orange endpoint. Calculate the phenolphthalein alkalinity, total alkalinity, and the concentration of hydroxides, carbonates, and bicarbonates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate P (Phenolphthalein Alkalinity):** (1 mark)
$$P = \frac{6.5 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100} = \frac{6500}{100} = 65 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate T (Total Alkalinity):** (1 mark)
$$T = \frac{(6.5 + 13.5) \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100} = \frac{20{,}000}{100} = 200 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3 — Apply species distribution rule:** (1 mark)
$$T/2 = 200/2 = 100 \text{ mg/L}$$
$$P = 65 \text{ mg/L} < 100 \text{ mg/L} = T/2$$
Therefore: **Case 2 (P < T/2)** applies.

**Step 4 — Calculate species concentrations:** (4 marks)
$$\text{OH}^- = 0 \text{ mg/L}$$
$$\text{CO}_3^{2-} = 2P = 2 \times 65 = 130 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$
$$\text{HCO}_3^- = T - 2P = 200 - 130 = 70 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Verification:** $0 + 130 + 70 = 200$ mg/L = T ✓

**Final Result:**
- P = 65 mg/L
- T = 200 mg/L
- OH⁻ = 0 mg/L
- CO₃²⁻ = 130 mg/L
- HCO₃⁻ = 70 mg/L

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the determination of acidity of a wastewater sample. Mention the indicators used and their significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (1 mark):**
Acidity is determined by titrating the wastewater sample with a standard strong base (0.02 N NaOH) to two pH endpoints, analogous to alkalinity titration but in the reverse direction.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Pipette 50 mL (or 100 mL) of wastewater sample into a 250 mL conical flask.
2. **Phenolphthalein acidity:** Add 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein. If colourless, titrate with 0.02 N NaOH until a faint pink colour persists for 30 seconds. Record $V_p$.
3. **Methyl orange acidity:** To the same flask, add 2–3 drops of methyl orange (solution turns red). Continue titration with NaOH until the colour changes to yellow/orange. For samples with high CO₂, boil for 2 minutes, cool, and re-titrate. Record total volume $V_t$.

**Indicators and significance (2 marks):**
- **Phenolphthalein (pH 8.3):** Measures strong mineral acidity.
- **Methyl orange (pH 4.5):** Measures total acidity including weak acids and CO₂.

**Calculation (1 mark):**
$$\text{Acidity (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$$

**(b)** 50 mL of an industrial wastewater sample required 15 mL of 0.1 N NaOH to reach the methyl orange endpoint. Calculate the total acidity and comment on whether the sample requires treatment before disposal into a municipal sewer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Acidity:** (2 marks)
$$T = \frac{15 \times 0.1 \times 50{,}000}{50} = \frac{75{,}000}{50} = 1500 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2 — Compare with disposal standards:** (2 marks)
- BIS/CPCB permissible limit for industrial discharge into municipal sewers: **pH 5.5–9.0** (corresponds roughly to acidity < 1000 mg/L or alkalinity < 1000 mg/L as CaCO₃ for safe discharge).
- Sample acidity = **1500 mg/L** >> 1000 mg/L → **Non-compliant**.

**Step 3 — Recommendation:** (3 marks)
The wastewater is **highly acidic** (likely from industrial acid washing, mining, or chemical manufacturing effluent). It requires:
1. **Neutralization treatment** — Lime (Ca(OH)₂) or NaOH dosing in an equalization/neutralization tank.
2. **pH adjustment** to 6.5–7.5 before discharge.
3. **Online pH monitoring** at the discharge point.
4. **Pre-treatment to remove heavy metals** (often co-present in acidic industrial waste).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this experiment — read carefully!**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion (2 marks lost)**: Students write $50$ instead of $50{,}000$ in the formula. The factor $50{,}000$ comes from $50$ (equivalent weight) $\times 1000$ (mg/g conversion). Memorize: **"50 thousand"** is the magic number for CaCO₃ reporting.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Species Branch (3 marks lost)**: In the species distribution table, students often pick the wrong row. Always compare $P$ with $T/2$ **BEFORE** assigning species. A common error is computing $2P$ when actually $P = 0$.
> 
> 3. **Endpoint Misreading (1–2 marks lost)**: For phenolphthalein, the endpoint is the **disappearance** of pink (not its first appearance in faint pink). For methyl orange, the endpoint is **orange/salmon** — NOT yellow, NOT red.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Acids and Bases in the Same Flask (2 marks lost)**: When asked to determine both alkalinity and acidity on the SAME sample, students sometimes titrate with both — this is wrong. **Alkalinity uses acid; acidity uses base**; they are two separate titrations on separate aliquots.
> 
> 5. **Sample Volume Inconsistency**: Always use the same $V_s$ (50 mL or 100 mL) for both P and T calculations. Don't accidentally use 50 mL for one and 100 mL for the other.
> 
> 6. **Blank Titration Ignored**: If the deionized water/indicator shows a slight endpoint shift, perform a blank titration and subtract the blank volume $V_b$ from $V_t$. Many students forget this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

### Key Definitions
- ☐ **Alkalinity** = Capacity to neutralize acid (mg/L as CaCO₃)
- ☐ **Acidity** = Capacity to neutralize base (mg/L as CaCO₃)

### Critical Indicators
- ☐ **Phenolphthalein endpoint** = pH **8.3**
- ☐ **Methyl orange endpoint** = pH **4.5**

### Universal Formula
- ☐ $\text{Conc. (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_s}$
- ☐ Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50**

### Titrants
- ☐ Alkalinity → **0.02 N H₂SO₄** (or HCl)
- ☐ Acidity → **0.02 N NaOH**

### Species Distribution Master Table
- ☐ $P = 0$ → Only HCO₃⁻
- ☐ $P < T/2$ → CO₃²⁻ = 2P, HCO₃⁻ = T − 2P
- ☐ $P = T/2$ → Only CO₃²⁻ (= T)
- ☐ $P > T/2$ → OH⁻ = 2P − T, CO₃²⁻ = 2(T − P)
- ☐ $P = T$ → Only OH⁻

### Procedure Reminders
- ☐ Always titrate in the **SAME conical flask** for P and T
- ☐ **2–3 drops** of indicator (not 5–6)
- ☐ **Swirl continuously** while titrating
- ☐ Place flask on a **white tile** for clear endpoint visualization
- ☐ **Boil and cool** wastewater samples with high CO₂ for accurate acidity

### Engineering Significance
- ☐ Alkalinity **> 500 mg/L** causes scale in boilers
- ☐ Alkalinity **< 50 mg/L** causes corrosive water
- ☐ Acidity **> 1000 mg/L** requires neutralization before discharge
- ☐ Drinking water pH range: **6.5–8.5** (IS 10500)
- ☐ Alkalinity = buffer against acid rain / pH shock

### Reporting & Calculation
- ☐ Always report in **mg/L as CaCO₃** (NOT as HCO₃⁻ or OH⁻)
- ☐ Show the **formula substitution** step in your exam answer
- ☐ Carry out **verification**: $OH^- + CO_3^{2-} + HCO_3^- = T$ mg/L
- ☐ Perform **blank titration** for accurate results

### Common Exam Patterns (KTU 2024)
- ☐ Numerical problem asking P, T, and species distribution
- ☐ Procedure explanation with indicator role
- ☐ Short note on engineering significance
- ☐ Comparison table between alkalinity and acidity
- ☐ Define terms, state causes, list sources

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Alkalinity & Acidity**
> *Practice the worked examples, memorize the species distribution table, and you will secure full marks in both the lab report and the university exam.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Hardness in Water and Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hardness in Water and Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Hardness** is defined as the total concentration of divalent (and higher valency) metallic cations — primarily **Calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **Magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions — expressed in terms of equivalent **Calcium Carbonate (CaCO₃)** in mg/L. The determination is carried out using the **Complexometric Titration method** with **Ethylenediaminetetraacetic Acid (EDTA)** as the titrant, employing **Eriochrome Black-T (EBT)** indicator for total hardness and **Murexide (Ammonium Purpurate)** for calcium hardness alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Reference):** Hardness is the capacity of water to precipitate soap, caused mainly by Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts. Total Hardness (TH) = Ca²⁺ Hardness + Mg²⁺ Hardness, expressed uniformly as mg/L CaCO₃.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to wash your hands with soap in a bucket of water. In **soft water** (rainwater, distilled water), the soap lathers immediately and cleans effectively. In **hard water** (groundwater rich in Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺), the soap does **not lather** — instead, it forms a sticky, white, curd-like scum (calcium/magnesium stearate) that floats on the surface and stains your clothes. 

The reason: the Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions "kidnap" the soap molecules before they can clean. We need a chemical "**detective**" (EDTA) that can **grab** these metallic ions and form a soluble, invisible complex — effectively "neutralizing" them so the soap can work. The detective holds the culprit ions in a tight claw-like structure (a **chelate complex**), and we detect the exact moment the detective has finished its job using a special color-changing witness (**indicator**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Board Examiner's Language):**
> - **Titrant** = EDTA solution of known normality (kept in the burette).
> - **Titrand / Analyte** = Water sample whose hardness is unknown.
> - **Indicator** = Eriochrome Black-T (wine red → steel blue at endpoint).
> - **Buffer Solution** = NH₄Cl + NH₄OH (pH ≈ 10 ± 0.1) — **MANDATORY** for total hardness.
> - **Masking Agent** = NaOH (pH ≈ 12 to 12.5) — used for Ca²⁺ alone; Mg²⁺ precipitates as Mg(OH)₂ and is removed from titration.

### 1.3 Hardness Classification (BIS / WHO Standards)

| Classification | Range (mg/L as CaCO₃) | Real-World Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Soft | **0 – 60** | Distilled water, rainwater |
| Moderately Hard | **61 – 120** | Many river waters (Kerala backwaters mostly soft) |
| Hard | **121 – 180** | Most groundwater in calcareous regions |
| Very Hard | **> 180** | Borewell water in limestone belts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard permissible limit for **total hardness in drinking water (IS 10500:2012)** is **200 mg/L (as CaCO₃)**, with an allowable limit up to **600 mg/L** in absence of alternate source. Anything above this causes scaling in boilers, pipe corrosion, and soap wastage.

### 1.4 Forms of Hardness

1. **Carbonate Hardness (Temporary Hardness):** Caused by bicarbonates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ — removable by **boiling** (precipitates as carbonate scale).
   $$\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

2. **Non-Carbonate Hardness (Permanent Hardness):** Caused by chlorides, sulphates, and nitrates of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ — **not** removed by boiling; requires ion-exchange or lime-soda softening.
   $$\text{CaSO}_4, \ \text{CaCl}_2, \ \text{MgSO}_4 \ \text{(permanent)}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of EDTA Complexometric Titration

**EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic Acid)** is a tetraprotic weak acid (H₄Y) that, in its fully deprotonated form (Y⁴⁻), acts as a **hexadentate ligand** — wrapping around a single metal ion through **two nitrogen atoms and four oxygen atoms (carboxylate groups)**, forming a stable **1:1 metal-ligand chelate** ring.

The central reaction (for both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ at pH 10):
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Y}^{4-} \longrightarrow [\text{CaY}]^{2-} \quad (\log K_f = 10.7)$$

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{Y}^{4-} \longrightarrow [\text{MgY}]^{2-} \quad (\log K_f = 8.7)$$

Because the equilibrium constant of **CaY²⁻ is much higher (10.7 vs 8.7)**, Ca²⁺ reacts with EDTA **first**, then Mg²⁺.

### 2.2 The Critical Role of pH and Buffer

- At **pH 10 ± 0.1** (ammonia buffer): both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ remain soluble and form complexes with EDTA → gives **Total Hardness**.
- At **pH 12 – 12.5** (NaOH alone): Mg²⁺ precipitates as **Mg(OH)₂** (white gelatinous precipitate) and is **excluded** from titration → gives **Calcium Hardness alone**.
- Below pH 10: complex is too weak (H⁺ competes with metal for EDTA); above pH 12: Ca(OH)₂ may also precipitate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indicator Behavior (KTU Favorite Question):**
> - **Eriochrome Black-T (EBT)** forms a weak wine-red complex with Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.
> - EDTA strips the metal from EBT (because CaY²⁻ is far more stable than Ca-EBT).
> - The **free EBT** anion imparts a **steel-blue color** — this is the **endpoint**.
> - At the endpoint: **Wine Red → Steel Blue** (this color change is sharpest at pH 10).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> **Standard EDTA Strength used in KTU Labs:** **0.01 M (or N/50)** — i.e., 1 mL ≡ 1.0 mg of CaCO₃ equivalent hardness.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normality of EDTA solution | $N_{\text{EDTA}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{CaCO}_3} \times 1000}{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50}$ | eq/L |
| 2 | **Total Hardness (TH)** | $\text{TH} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA(TH)}} \times N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 3 | **Calcium Hardness (CH)** | $\text{CH} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA(CH)}} \times N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 4 | **Magnesium Hardness (MH)** | $\text{MH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 5 | Hardness in terms of Ca²⁺ (mg/L) | $\text{Ca}^{2+} = \text{CH} \times 0.4$ | mg/L |
| 6 | Hardness in terms of Mg²⁺ (mg/L) | $\text{Mg}^{2+} = \text{MH} \times 0.243$ | mg/L |
| 7 | Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ | 50 g/eq | — |
| 8 | Molecular weight of CaCO₃ | 100 g/mol | — |
| 9 | Atomic weight Ca | **40 g/mol** | — |
| 10 | Atomic weight Mg | **24.3 g/mol** | — |

> **Why factor 50 in formula?** The equivalent weight of CaCO₃ is **50 g/eq** (= 100/2). This converts normality (eq/L) into mass of CaCO₃, so that hardness of any cation is reported uniformly "as CaCO₃."

### 2.4 Conversion Logic — Why Express as CaCO₃?

A 1 mg/L Ca²⁺ solution and a 1 mg/L Mg²⁺ solution do **not** have the same hardness. To make them comparable, we convert to an equivalent mass of CaCO₃:

$$\text{Ca}^{2+}: \quad \text{1 mg Ca}^{2+} = \text{1} \times \frac{100}{40} = \text{2.5 mg CaCO}_3$$

$$\text{Mg}^{2+}: \quad \text{1 mg Mg}^{2+} = \text{1} \times \frac{100}{24.3} = \text{4.12 mg CaCO}_3$$

These multipliers (2.5 and 4.12) are the inverse of the factors (0.4 and 0.243) used in the cheat sheet above. **This is a very common KTU 3-mark question.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Boiler Feed Water (IS 10392):** TH must be **< 1 mg/L** for high-pressure boilers to prevent scale formation (1 mm scale = ~7% fuel loss).
2. **Cooling Towers:** TH controlled to **< 500 mg/L** to prevent condenser fouling.
3. **Swimming Pools:** Maintain 200 – 400 mg/L for bather comfort.
4. **Reverse Osmosis (RO) Pretreatment:** Hardness removal prolongs membrane life.
5. **Textile Industry:** Hard water causes uneven dyeing and fabric stiffening.
6. **Pharmaceutical Water (Purified Water IP):** TH < 1 mg/L mandatory.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Hardness Formula

We start from the definition of **normality**:
$$N = \frac{\text{Number of equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}$$

For a titration, the **law of equivalence** states that at the endpoint:
$$\text{Equivalents of EDTA} = \text{Equivalents of hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)}$$

$$N_{\text{EDTA}} \times V_{\text{EDTA}} \, (\text{L}) = N_{\text{hardness}} \times V_{\text{sample}} \, (\text{L})$$

Since hardness is reported "as CaCO₃", the normality of hardness (in eq/L of CaCO₃ equivalent) is:
$$N_{\text{hardness}} = \frac{\text{mass of CaCO}_3 \,(\text{g/L})}{50} = \frac{\text{TH (mg/L)}}{50 \times 1000}$$

Substituting:
$$N_{\text{EDTA}} \times V_{\text{EDTA}} = \frac{\text{TH (mg/L)}}{50 \times 1000} \times V_{\text{sample}} \,(\text{mL})$$

Solving for TH:
$$\text{TH (mg/L)} = \frac{N_{\text{EDTA}} \times V_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

Since $N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 = \text{EDTA titre factor} \times 1$ (for 0.01 M EDTA, $N = 0.02$, so $N \times 50 = 1$), the simplified working formula becomes:

$$\boxed{\text{TH (mg/L as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \frac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \,(\text{mL}) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}} \,(\text{mL})}}$$

when 1 mL EDTA ≡ 1 mg CaCO₃. **This is the formula KTU students must memorize.**

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical (Sample Problem — KTU 2023 Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:** A 50 mL water sample required **12.4 mL** of **0.01 M EDTA** for total hardness, and another 50 mL sample required **8.2 mL** of the same EDTA for calcium hardness (using NaOH + murexide). Calculate TH, CH, MH in mg/L as CaCO₃, and the actual Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ concentrations in mg/L. Classify the water.

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{EDTA, TH}} = 12.4$ mL
- $V_{\text{EDTA, CH}} = 8.2$ mL
- $V_{\text{sample}} = 50$ mL
- EDTA: 0.01 M → 1 mL ≡ 1.0 mg CaCO₃

#### Step 1: Total Hardness (TH)

$$\text{TH} = \frac{V_{\text{EDTA, TH}} \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{12.4 \times 1000}{50}$$

$$\text{TH} = \frac{12400}{50} = 248 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

#### Step 2: Calcium Hardness (CH)

$$\text{CH} = \frac{8.2 \times 1000}{50} = \frac{8200}{50} = 164 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

#### Step 3: Magnesium Hardness (MH)

$$\text{MH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH} = 248 - 164 = 84 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

#### Step 4: Actual Ca²⁺ Concentration (mg/L)

Conversion factor from CaCO₃ to Ca²⁺ = $\dfrac{40}{100} = 0.4$

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} = 164 \times 0.4 = 65.6 \, \text{mg/L}$$

#### Step 5: Actual Mg²⁺ Concentration (mg/L)

Conversion factor from CaCO₃ to Mg²⁺ = $\dfrac{24.3}{100} = 0.243$

$$\text{Mg}^{2+} = 84 \times 0.243 = 20.4 \, \text{mg/L}$$

#### Step 6: Classification

Since TH = **248 mg/L** falls in the range **121–180 mg/L**? No — actually 248 is **> 180 mg/L** → **Very Hard Water**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check by atomic mass balance:** 
> Ca²⁺ equivalents: 65.6/20 = 3.28 meq/L
> Mg²⁺ equivalents: 20.4/12.15 = 1.68 meq/L
> Total meq/L = 4.96 ≈ TH/50 = 248/50 = 4.96 ✓ (Confirms calculation)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Total Hardness, Ca Hardness, Mg Hardness

| Parameter | Total Hardness (TH) | Calcium Hardness (CH) | Magnesium Hardness (MH) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indicator used** | Eriochrome Black-T (EBT) | Murexide / Patton-Reeder | Calculated (TH − CH) |
| **Buffer / pH** | NH₄Cl + NH₄OH (pH 10 ± 0.1) | NaOH pellets/solution (pH 12–12.5) | — |
| **Masking of Mg²⁺?** | No (both titrated) | Yes (Mg(OH)₂ precipitates) | — |
| **Color Change** | Wine Red → Steel Blue | Pink → Purple (or Salmon → Blue) | — |
| **Volume of EDTA (mL)** | $V_1$ | $V_2$ | $V_1 - V_2$ |
| **Titration pH Stability** | Must be strictly 10 ± 0.1 | Must be > 12 (use NaOH) | — |
| **KTU Board Marks (out of 14)** | 7 | 4 | 3 |

### 3.4 Standardization of EDTA — Derivation

EDTA is **never a primary standard** (it is hygroscopic, contains impurities). It is standardized against **primary standard CaCO₃** (analytical reagent grade, dried at 110°C for 2 h).

Procedure: Dissolve exactly **1.0 g CaCO₃** in minimum HCl, dilute to 1 L → 1 mL = **1.0 mg CaCO₃** (1000 mg/L).

Pipette 25 mL of this standard → titrate with EDTA using EBT at pH 10.

$$N_{\text{EDTA}} = \frac{25 \times 1.0 \times \dfrac{1000}{1000}}{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50} = \frac{0.5}{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50}$$

If $V_{\text{EDTA}} = 24.8$ mL:
$$N_{\text{EDTA}} = \frac{0.5}{24.8 \times 50} = 0.000403 \, \text{N} \approx \frac{1}{50} \, N$$

This confirms 1 mL EDTA ≈ 1 mg CaCO₃ equivalent.

> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the **2 H⁺** released during complexation at pH 10, which is why the **buffer is mandatory** — to neutralize the H⁺ and prevent pH drop, otherwise the complex would dissociate before endpoint.

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons [\text{CaY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EDTA Titration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Water Sample] --> B[Measure 50 mL in Conical Flask]
    B --> C{Which Hardness?}
    C -->|Total Hardness TH| D[Add 1 mL Ammonia Buffer pH 10]
    C -->|Calcium Hardness CH| E[Add 1 mL 8N NaOH pH 12.5]
    D --> F[Add 2-3 drops EBT Indicator]
    E --> G[Add Murexide Indicator]
    F --> H[Wine Red Solution]
    G --> I[Pink/Salmon Solution]
    H --> J[Titrate with 0.01 M EDTA from Burette]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Endpoint Reached?}
    K -->|No Wine Red| J
    K -->|Steel Blue Color| L[Note V1 mL for TH]
    K -->|Purple Color| M[Note V2 mL for CH]
    L --> N[Compute TH mg/L]
    M --> O[Compute CH mg/L]
    N --> P[Compute MH = TH - CH]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Classify Water Soft Moderate Hard Very Hard]
    Q --> R[Compare with IS 10500 Standard 200 mg/L]
    R --> S[End: Report Hardness in mg/L as CaCO3]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Hardness Analysis System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sample_Prep[Sample Preparation Unit]
        S1[Raw Water Sample] --> S2[Filter through Whatman 42]
        S2 --> S3[Measure 50 mL Aliquot]
    end

    subgraph Reaction_Chamber[Reaction Chemistry]
        S3 --> R1[Buffer Addition pH 10 or 12]
        R1 --> R2[Indicator EBT or Murexide]
        R2 --> R3[Metal Indicator Complex M-In Wine Red]
    end

    subgraph Titration_Unit[Titration Module]
        R3 --> T1[EDTA from Burette 0.01 M]
        T1 --> T2[Complexation: M2+ + Y4- = MY2-]
        T2 --> T3{All Metal Titrated?}
        T3 -->|No Continue| T1
        T3 -->|Yes EDTA strips M from Indicator| T4[Free Indicator Steel Blue]
    end

    subgraph Computation_Engine[Calculation Engine]
        T4 --> C1[Record V_EDTA mL]
        C1 --> C2[Apply Formula TH = V x 1000 x N x 50 / Vsample]
        C2 --> C3[Compute CH and MH]
        C3 --> C4[Classify and Compare with Standards]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — pH-Dependent Selective Titration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Hardness Cations in Water] --> Q{pH Control}

    Q -->|pH 10 NH3 Buffer| R1[Both Ca2+ and Mg2+ Soluble]
    Q -->|pH 12 NaOH| R2[Mg OH 2 Precipitates White]

    R1 --> S1[EDTA Titration Titrates Ca + Mg]
    R2 --> S2[EDTA Titration Titrates Ca Only]

    S1 --> X1[Total Hardness TH]
    S2 --> X2[Calcium Hardness CH]

    X1 --> Y[Magnesium Hardness MH = TH - CH]
    X2 --> Y
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term 'hardness of water'. Why is it expressed in terms of CaCO₃ equivalent?**

**Model Answer:**
Hardness of water is defined as the total concentration of divalent cations, primarily **calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions, present in water. It is the soap-consuming capacity of water and causes scale formation in boilers.

It is expressed in terms of **CaCO₃ equivalent** because:
1. CaCO₃ has a molecular weight of **100 g/mol** and equivalent weight of **50 g/eq** — convenient for calculation.
2. It provides a **uniform reference** to compare the precipitation power of different cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe²⁺, etc.).
3. The equivalent concentrations of different ions can be directly added to obtain total hardness.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between carbonate hardness and non-carbonate hardness. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Carbonate Hardness (Temporary) | Non-Carbonate Hardness (Permanent) |
|---|---|---|
| Anions involved | Bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻) and Carbonates (CO₃²⁻) | Chlorides, Sulphates, Nitrates |
| Removal method | Boiling (precipitates as scale) | Ion-exchange, Lime-soda process |
| Example | Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂ | CaSO₄, MgCl₂, CaCl₂ |
| Causes boiler scale | Yes (CaCO₃) | Yes (CaSO₄ — harder scale) |
| Effect on soap | Yes | Yes |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Experiment 2: Determination of Hardness in Water and Wastewater**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **principle of complexometric titration** of total hardness using **EDTA**. State the role of buffer, indicator, and the chemical reactions involved. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) in its deprotonated form (Y⁴⁻) reacts with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ to form **soluble 1:1 chelate complexes** [CaY]²⁻ and [MgY]²⁻. The stability constants are log K = 10.7 (Ca) and 8.7 (Mg).

**Role of Buffer (NH₄Cl + NH₄OH, pH 10):**
- Maintains pH at **10 ± 0.1** to keep Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ in solution.
- Neutralizes the 2 H⁺ released during complexation, preventing pH drop.
- At pH 10, the conditional stability constant of CaY²⁻ is highest.

**Role of Indicator (Eriochrome Black-T, EBT):**
- Forms a weak wine-red complex with Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺: M²⁺ + EBT → [M-EBT] (wine red).
- EDTA, having a much higher affinity for the metal, strips it from EBT.
- At endpoint, **free EBT** is liberated, imparting a **steel-blue color**.

**Reactions:**
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\text{pH 10}} [\text{CaY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \xrightarrow{\text{pH 10}} [\text{MgY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{Endpoint: [Ca-EBT] (wine red) + EDTA → [CaY]}^{2-} + \text{free EBT (steel blue)}$$

**(b)** A 100 mL water sample required **15.2 mL of 0.01 M EDTA** for total hardness. Another 100 mL sample required **9.4 mL of the same EDTA** for calcium hardness using murexide indicator. Calculate the total, calcium, and magnesium hardness in **mg/L as CaCO₃**, and the actual Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ in **mg/L**. Classify the water. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_1$ (TH) = 15.2 mL, $V_2$ (CH) = 9.4 mL, $V_{\text{sample}} = 100$ mL, EDTA = 0.01 M (1 mL ≡ 1 mg CaCO₃)

**Step 1: Total Hardness**
$$\text{TH} = \frac{V_1 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{15.2 \times 1000}{100} = 152 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2: Calcium Hardness**
$$\text{CH} = \frac{V_2 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{9.4 \times 1000}{100} = 94 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3: Magnesium Hardness**
