$$\text{MH} = \text{TH} - \text{CH} = 152 - 94 = 58 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4: Actual Ca²⁺ (mg/L)**
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} = 94 \times 0.4 = 37.6 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5: Actual Mg²⁺ (mg/L)**
$$\text{Mg}^{2+} = 58 \times 0.243 = 14.09 \, \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 6: Classification**
TH = 152 mg/L → falls in **121 – 180 mg/L** → **Hard Water** (per IS classification).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **laboratory procedure** for the determination of total hardness, calcium hardness, and magnesium hardness in a water sample. List the apparatus, reagents, and indicators used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:**
- Burette (50 mL), pipette (50 mL), conical flasks (250 mL × 3)
- Measuring cylinder, wash bottle, white tile
- Standard 0.01 M EDTA solution, NH₄Cl-NH₄OH buffer (pH 10), 8N NaOH

**Reagents:**
1. **0.01 M EDTA solution** standardized against CaCO₃ (1 mL ≡ 1 mg CaCO₃)
2. **Ammonia buffer (pH 10):** 16.9 g NH₄Cl + 143 mL NH₄OH diluted to 250 mL
3. **Eriochrome Black-T (EBT)** indicator — 0.5 g in 100 mL triethanolamine
4. **Murexide indicator** — 0.2 g in 100 mL water (or ammonium purpurate + NaCl)
5. **8N NaOH solution**

**Procedure for Total Hardness:**
1. Pipette **50 mL** of well-mixed water sample into a clean 250 mL conical flask.
2. Add **1 mL of ammonia buffer** (pH 10).
3. Add **2–3 drops of EBT indicator** → solution turns **wine red**.
4. Titrate against **0.01 M EDTA** from burette with continuous swirling.
5. The endpoint is the **first appearance of steel-blue color** persisting for 30 seconds.
6. Note the volume $V_1$ mL.

**Procedure for Calcium Hardness:**
1. Take another **50 mL sample** in a fresh flask.
2. Add **2 mL of 8N NaOH** (pH 12.5) → Mg(OH)₂ precipitates as white gelatinous mass.
3. Add **0.1 g of murexide indicator** (or 2 drops solution) → solution turns **salmon pink**.
4. Titrate with **0.01 M EDTA** → endpoint is **purple/violet** color.
5. Note the volume $V_2$ mL.

**Magnesium Hardness = Total Hardness − Calcium Hardness (in terms of EDTA volume: $V_1 - V_2$).**

**(b)** A groundwater sample gave the following results:
- Standardization: 1 mL EDTA ≡ 1.05 mg CaCO₃
- Total Hardness titration: 25.0 mL sample required **8.5 mL EDTA**
- Calcium Hardness titration: 25.0 mL sample required **5.2 mL EDTA**

Calculate TH, CH, MH (in mg/L as CaCO₃), and the hardness in **French degrees (°f)** and **Clarke's degrees (°Cl)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Titre factor = 1.05 mg CaCO₃/mL EDTA, $V_1$ = 8.5 mL, $V_2$ = 5.2 mL, $V_{\text{sample}}$ = 25 mL

**Step 1: TH in mg/L as CaCO₃**
$$\text{TH} = \frac{V_1 \times \text{ titre factor} \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{8.5 \times 1.05 \times 1000}{25}$$
$$\text{TH} = \frac{8925}{25} = 357 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 2: CH in mg/L as CaCO₃**
$$\text{CH} = \frac{5.2 \times 1.05 \times 1000}{25} = \frac{5460}{25} = 218.4 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 3: MH in mg/L as CaCO₃**
$$\text{MH} = 357 - 218.4 = 138.6 \, \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 4: Convert to French Degrees (°f)**
1 °f = 10 mg/L CaCO₃
$$\text{TH (°f)} = \frac{357}{10} = 35.7 \, \text{°f}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Clarke's Degrees (°Cl)**
1 °Cl = 14.3 mg/L CaCO₃ (1 grain CaCO₃ per Imperial gallon = 14.3 mg/L)
$$\text{TH (°Cl)} = \frac{357}{14.3} = 24.97 \approx 25 \, \text{°Cl}$$

> **Additional (Bonus): German Degree (°dH):** TH = 357/17.9 = 19.94 °dH (1 °dH = 17.9 mg/L CaCO₃).

**Classification:** TH = 357 mg/L → **Very Hard Water**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing TH and CH indicators:** Many students write EBT for calcium hardness. The correct indicator for **CH is murexide** (or Patton-Reeder), and EBT is for TH only. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting pH control:** Not mentioning the buffer pH 10 for TH or NaOH pH 12.5 for CH. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Unit errors:** Writing hardness in mg/L as Ca²⁺ instead of mg/L as CaCO₃ in the final answer. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Wrong conversion factor:** Using 0.243 and 0.4 incorrectly (or reversing them). **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Standardization omitted:** In numerical problems, students often forget that EDTA titre factor (≠ 1.00) must be used when EDTA is not exactly 0.01 M. **(−2 marks)**
> 6. **Indicator color change:** Writing "blue to red" instead of "**red to blue**" at endpoint. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Mg²⁺ precipitation in CH titration:** Failing to mention that Mg²⁺ precipitates as Mg(OH)₂ at pH 12.5 and is **excluded** from titration. **(−2 marks)**
> 8. **Blank titration not subtracted:** Some practical scenarios require a blank correction — omitting this loses accuracy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Hardness = Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ content, expressed as **mg/L CaCO₃**.
- ☐ **EDTA:** Hexadentate ligand; 1:1 complex; titrant used is **0.01 M** (1 mL ≡ 1 mg CaCO₃).
- ☐ **Indicators:** **EBT** (wine red → steel blue, pH 10) for TH; **Murexide** (pink → purple, pH 12) for CH.
- ☐ **Buffer for TH:** NH₄Cl + NH₄OH at **pH 10 ± 0.1**.
- ☐ **Masking for CH:** NaOH at **pH 12 – 12.5**; Mg(OH)₂ precipitates.
- ☐ **Master Formula:** 
   TH (mg/L) = (V_EDTA × titre factor × 1000) / V_sample
- ☐ **MH = TH − CH.**
- ☐ **Conversion to actual ions:** Ca²⁺ = CH × 0.4; Mg²⁺ = MH × 0.243.
- ☐ **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50.**
- ☐ **Limit (IS 10500):** 200 mg/L (acceptable up to 600 mg/L) for drinking water.
- ☐ **Hardness Units:** French (°f = 10 mg/L), Clarke (°Cl = 14.3 mg/L), German (°dH = 17.9 mg/L).
- ☐ **At endpoint:** Color changes from **red → blue** (NOT blue → red).
- ☐ **EDTA is NOT a primary standard** — must be standardized against CaCO₃.
- ☐ **Two H⁺ released** during complexation — buffer is **mandatory**.
- ☐ **Temporary vs Permanent Hardness:** Boiling removes temporary (carbonate) hardness only.
- ☐ **Equation:** TH as CaCO₃ (mg/L) = Total meq/L × 50 = (Ca²⁺/20 + Mg²⁺/12.15) × 50.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner's Tip:** In viva voce, the most asked question is *"Why is the buffer solution added?"* — Answer: *"To maintain the pH at 10.0 ± 0.1 so that both Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ form stable 1:1 complexes with EDTA, and to neutralize the 2 H⁺ ions released during the chelation reaction."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Chloride in Water and Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Chloride in Water and Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chloride (Cl⁻)** is the most abundant inorganic anion present in natural waters, originating primarily from the dissolution of salt deposits, seawater intrusion, agricultural runoff, and industrial discharges (textile, food processing, and chlorinated chemical plants). In environmental engineering practice, the **Argentometric Method (Mohr's Method)** is the standard titrimetric procedure prescribed by **Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (APHA, 23rd Edition, Method 4500-Cl⁻ B)** for the quantitative determination of chloride in drinking water, surface water, domestic wastewater, and industrial effluents within the concentration range of **0.15 to 10 mg Cl⁻ (when a 100 mL sample is titrated with 0.0141 N AgNO₃ to a 5 mL endpoint)**.

The method is based on the **precipitation of chloride ion with silver nitrate (AgNO₃) titrant in a neutral or slightly alkaline medium**, using **potassium chromate (K₂CrO₄) as the visual indicator**, which forms a brick-red precipitate of silver chromate (Ag₂CrO₄) at the equivalence point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mohr's method (Argentometric titration) is the **prescribed experiment** under Module 2 of PCCVL508. Students must be proficient in the principle, indicator chemistry, titration procedure, calculation methodology, and the significance of chloride in water quality assessment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a dance floor where two types of dancers compete for a single partner:
- The **chloride ions (Cl⁻)** in the water sample are the "primary partners" — abundant and eager.
- The **silver ions (Ag⁺)** from the AgNO₃ titrant are "secondary partners" being added dropwise.

Since **silver chloride (AgCl)** is *much less soluble* than **silver chromate (Ag₂CrO₄)**, every silver ion introduced preferentially "dances" with a chloride ion first, forming a white curdy precipitate of AgCl. The **chromate indicator sits patiently in the background**, waiting.

The moment **all the chloride ions are paired** (i.e., the equivalence point is reached), the next drop of silver ion has no chloride left. So it grabs the chromate instead, instantly turning the solution **brick-red/pinkish-brown** — like a referee blowing a whistle to signal "all chloride ions have been claimed!"

The number of silver drops used is mathematically proportional to the number of chloride ions initially present.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Cue:** The transition from a **pale yellow** solution (chromate indicator) to a **persistent brick-red endpoint** (Ag₂CrO₄) signifies completion of the reaction. The endpoint color must persist for **at least 30 seconds** to be valid.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Equivalent weight of Chloride (Cl⁻):** **35.45 g/eq**
- **Molar mass of AgNO₃:** **169.87 g/mol**
- **Standard normality of AgNO₃ titrant:** **0.0141 N** (or 0.1 N for highly concentrated samples)
- **Indicator concentration:** **5% w/v K₂CrO₄** solution
- **Ksp of AgCl:** **1.76 × 10⁻¹⁰** (at 25 °C)
- **Ksp of Ag₂CrO₄:** **1.10 × 10⁻¹²** (at 25 °C)
- **Acceptable pH range:** **7.0 – 8.5** (critical boundary condition)
- **Standard reference temperature:** **25 °C**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Chemical Principle

Mohr's method is a **precipitation titration** governed by the **relative solubility product (Ksp)** differences between two sparingly soluble silver salts. The order of precipitation follows the **Ksp-based solubility hierarchy** (the salt with the *lower* Ksp precipitates *first*).

### 2.1.1 Primary Precipitation Reaction (with analyte)

$$ \text{Ag}^+_{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} \downarrow \;\; (\text{white}) $$

This reaction is the **stoichiometric, quantitative** reaction of interest. For every mole of Cl⁻ consumed, exactly **one mole of Ag⁺** is consumed.

### 2.1.2 Indicator Reaction (at the endpoint)

$$ 2\text{Ag}^+_{(aq)} + \text{CrO}_4^{2-}_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 \downarrow \;\; (\text{brick red}) $$

The Ag₂CrO₄ precipitate appears only **after all Cl⁻ has been consumed**, because AgCl has a *lower* Ksp than Ag₂CrO₄. The chromate ion essentially acts as a "spectator indicator" that responds to the **excess** Ag⁺ beyond the equivalence point.

### 2.1.3 The 'Why' Behind the Method

- **Why AgNO₃?** Silver forms highly insoluble precipitates with halides. AgCl is the least expensive and most stable halide precipitate.
- **Why K₂CrO₄ as indicator?** Ag₂CrO₄ has a *distinctive color* (brick red) that contrasts sharply with white AgCl, providing a sharp visual endpoint.
- **Why pH 7.0 – 8.5?**
  - At pH < 6.5, chromate converts to dichromate (Cr₂O₇²⁻), reducing indicator concentration and delaying/diminishing the endpoint.
  - At pH > 10.0, Ag⁺ precipitates as brown Ag₂O, causing early endpoint errors.
- **Why neutral/slightly alkaline?** The chromate indicator chemistry and silver stability are both pH-sensitive; the narrow pH window ensures reliable endpoint detection.

## 2.2 The 'How' — Step-Wise Logic of the Method

1. **Sample preparation:** Measure a known volume (V_s) of water/wastewater sample (typically 50 or 100 mL) into a clean conical flask.
2. **pH adjustment:** If required, neutralize the sample to pH 7–9 using NaHCO₃ powder (do not use NaOH, which can over-alkalinize).
3. **Indicator addition:** Add 1 mL of 5% K₂CrO₄ indicator solution → solution turns **lemon yellow**.
4. **Titration:** Titrate against standard AgNO₃ (0.0141 N) from a burette with continuous swirling.
5. **Endpoint detection:** A **persistent brick-red** color (due to Ag₂CrO₄ formation) marks the equivalence point.
6. **Blank correction:** Run a blank titration with distilled water + indicator (subtract this value from the sample titre).
7. **Calculation:** Apply the standard chloride formula (Section 2.4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Titration Etiquette:** Always read the **upper meniscus** for AgNO₃ (dark/colorless) and the **lower meniscus** for yellow solutions. Swirl continuously; do not add titrant in large slugs near the endpoint.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Chloride concentration | $\text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg/L | Primary KTU formula |
| 2 | Mass of AgNO₃ for 0.0141 N (per 1000 mL) | $0.0141 \times 169.87 = 2.396$ g | g | Dissolve in distilled water |
| 3 | Mass of AgNO₃ for 0.1 N (per 1000 mL) | $0.1 \times 169.87 = 16.987$ g | g | For high-strength wastewater |
| 4 | Indicator preparation (5% K₂CrO₄) | 5 g K₂CrO₄ in 100 mL distilled water | %w/v | Use neutral/alkaline salt |
| 5 | Sample volume (typical) | 50 mL or 100 mL | mL | Dilute if Cl⁻ > 500 mg/L |
| 6 | Dilution formula | $C_1 V_1 = C_2 V_2$ | mg/L, mL | If sample exceeds range |
| 7 | Blank subtraction | $V_{\text{corrected}} = V_{\text{sample}} - V_{\text{blank}}$ | mL | Always performed |
| 8 | Equivalent weight of Cl⁻ | 35.45 | g/eq | Standard atomic mass |
| 9 | Ksp of AgCl | $1.76 \times 10^{-10}$ | — | Lower Ksp → precipitates first |
| 10 | Ksp of Ag₂CrO₄ | $1.10 \times 10^{-12}$ | — | Higher Ksp → precipitates later |
| 11 | Acceptable pH | 7.0 – 8.5 | pH units | Critical boundary condition |
| 12 | Conversion to mg/L NaCl | $\text{NaCl} = \text{Cl}^- \times 1.65$ | mg/L | 1.65 = M(NaCl)/M(Cl) ratio |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chloride monitoring is indispensable in:
- **Drinking water treatment plants** — to control corrosion in distribution pipes (Cl⁻ > 250 mg/L causes pitting corrosion of stainless steel).
- **Boiler feed water quality control** — high chloride causes scaling and stress corrosion cracking.
- **Concrete structures** — Cl⁻ > 500 mg/L corrodes reinforced steel (rebar depassivation).
- **Agricultural irrigation water** — affects crop yield and soil salinity (Salinity Hazard, SAR).
- **Wastewater discharge compliance** — CPCB/SPCB stipulated limits under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974.
- **Desalination plant performance evaluation** — Cl⁻ is the primary rejection parameter for RO membranes.
- **Saline intrusion studies** in coastal aquifers.

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS/IS 10500:2012 Limit for Chloride in Drinking Water:** **250 mg/L** (acceptable limit) and **1000 mg/L** (permissible limit in absence of alternate source).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Chloride Concentration Formula

### Step 1: Statement of the Equivalence Condition

At the equivalence point of the Mohr's titration, the number of equivalents of AgNO₃ added equals the number of equivalents of Cl⁻ originally present in the sample.

$$ N_{\text{AgNO}_3} \times V_{\text{AgNO}_3} = N_{\text{Cl}^-} \times V_{\text{sample}} $$

### Step 2: Expressing Normality in Terms of Mass

By definition, normality is the number of gram-equivalents of solute per litre of solution.

$$ N_{\text{Cl}^-} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Cl}^- \; (\text{g})}{E_{\text{Cl}} \times V_{\text{sample}} \; (\text{L})} $$

where $E_{\text{Cl}} = 35.45$ g/eq is the equivalent weight of chloride.

### Step 3: Solving for Mass of Chloride

Rearranging the equivalence condition:

$$ \text{Mass of Cl}^- = N_{\text{AgNO}_3} \times V_{\text{AgNO}_3} \times E_{\text{Cl}} $$

Note: All volumes must be in the same unit (litres) for consistency.

### Step 4: Converting to mg/L (the Standard Environmental Reporting Unit)

$$ \text{Cl}^- \; (\text{mg/L}) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of Cl}^- \; (\text{mg})}{V_{\text{sample}} \; (\text{L})} $$

Substituting the previous expression (and converting grams to milligrams by multiplying by 1000):

$$ \text{Cl}^- \; (\text{mg/L}) = \dfrac{N_{\text{AgNO}_3} \times V_{\text{AgNO}_3} \; (\text{L}) \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}} \; (\text{L})} $$

### Step 5: Expressing Titrant Volume in mL (Laboratory Practical Unit)

To use the burette reading (in mL) directly:

$$ V_{\text{AgNO}_3} \; (\text{mL}) = \dfrac{V_{\text{AgNO}_3} \; (\text{L})}{1000} $$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$ \text{Cl}^- \; (\text{mg/L}) = \dfrac{N_{\text{AgNO}_3} \times V_{\text{AgNO}_3} \; (\text{mL}) \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}} \; (\text{mL}) \times 1000} $$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form (The KTU Master Formula)

$$ \boxed{\text{Cl}^- \; (\text{mg/L}) = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}} $$

where:
- $V_s$ = volume of AgNO₃ used for sample titration (mL)
- $V_b$ = volume of AgNO₃ used for blank titration (mL)
- $N$ = normality of AgNO₃ solution
- $35.45$ = equivalent weight of Cl⁻ (g/eq)
- $1000$ = conversion factor (mL to L for normality definition)
- $V_{\text{sample}}$ = volume of water/wastewater sample taken (mL)

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A water sample (50 mL) was titrated against 0.0141 N AgNO₃ using K₂CrO₄ indicator. The burette readings were: initial = 0.50 mL, final = 12.30 mL. A blank titration consumed 0.20 mL of AgNO₃. Calculate the chloride concentration in mg/L. Comment on its suitability for drinking purposes as per IS 10500:2012.

### Solution

**Step 1: Sample titre volume**

$$ V_s = V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}} = 12.30 - 0.50 = 11.80 \; \text{mL} $$

**Step 2: Blank correction**

$$ V_{\text{corrected}} = V_s - V_b = 11.80 - 0.20 = 11.60 \; \text{mL} $$

**Step 3: Apply the KTU formula**

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{V_{\text{corrected}} \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} $$

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{11.60 \times 0.0141 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{50} $$

**Step 4: Numerator evaluation (one step at a time)**

$$ 11.60 \times 0.0141 = 0.16356 $$

$$ 0.16356 \times 35.45 = 5.7982 $$

$$ 5.7982 \times 1000 = 5798.2 $$

**Step 5: Final division**

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{5798.2}{50} = 115.96 \; \text{mg/L} $$

**Step 6: Inference**

Since **115.96 mg/L < 250 mg/L** (the BIS acceptable limit), the water is **suitable for drinking** with respect to chloride content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU marks distribution for such a problem:** Procedure (3 marks) + Calculation (6 marks) + Interpretation/Comment (3 marks) + Units and significant figures (2 marks) = 14 marks total.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Chloride Determination Methods

| Parameter | Mohr's Method (AgNO₃) | Volhard's Method | Mercuric Nitrate Method | Potentiometric (Ion-selective) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Precipitation with AgNO₃ + chromate indicator | Back-titration with KSCN | Complexation with Hg(NO₃)₂ + diphenylcarbazone | EMF measurement using Ag/AgCl electrode |
| **Titrant** | AgNO₃ (direct) | AgNO₃ + KSCN (back) | Hg(NO₃)₂ | Standard AgNO₃ |
| **Indicator** | K₂CrO₄ (5%) | FeNH₄(SO₄)₂ (ferric alum) | Diphenylcarbazone | N/A (instrumental) |
| **pH Range** | 7.0 – 8.5 | Strongly acidic (HNO₃) | 2.3 – 2.8 | 2 – 11 |
| **Interferences** | Br⁻, I⁻, S²⁻, CN⁻, PO₄³⁻ | Few (more selective) | Few | Minimal (high selectivity) |
| **Color/Turbidity** | Disturbs endpoint | Tolerates turbidity | Tolerates turbidity | Best for colored samples |
| **Range (mg/L)** | 0.15 – 10 (100 mL sample) | 0.15 – 100 | 0.05 – 5 | 1 – 35000 |
| **APHA Reference** | 4500-Cl⁻ B | 4500-Cl⁻ C | 4500-Cl⁻ D | 4500-Cl⁻ D (modified) |
| **Suitability** | Routine drinking water | Wastewater | Standard for colorimetric | Field/continuous monitoring |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate (Hg waste) | High (instrumental) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mohr's Method Titration Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete sequential process** of the Mohr's method experiment, including sample collection, titration, calculation, and quality validation steps.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water or Wastewater Sample Collection] --> B[Measure Sample Volume Vs into Conical Flask]
    B --> C[Add 1 mL of 5 percent K2CrO4 Indicator]
    C --> D{Solution Pale Yellow?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Fill Burette with Standard AgNO3 0.0141 N]
    D -- No --> C
    E --> F[Titrate Dropwise with Constant Swirling]
    F --> G{Persistent Brick Red Color Appears?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Record Final Burette Reading Vf]
    H --> I[Perform Blank Titration with Distilled Water]
    I --> J[Subtract Blank Titre Vb from Sample Titre Vs]
    J --> K[Apply KTU Chloride Formula]
    K --> L[Cl minus mg per L equals Vs minus Vb times N times 35.45 times 1000 divided by Vsample]
    L --> M{Result less than 250 mg per L?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Sample Suitable for Drinking as per IS 10500]
    M -- No --> O[Sample Exceeds BIS Limit Further Treatment Required]
    N --> P[Report and Tabulate Results]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[End of Experiment]
```

## 4.2 Reaction Mechanism Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates the **parallel chemical reactions** occurring during the titration and the role of the chromate indicator as a "spectator" that only reacts at the equivalence point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ag plus from AgNO3 Titrant] --> B{Reaction Pathway 1}
    A --> C{Reaction Pathway 2}
    B --> D[AgCl White Precipitate with Cl minus]
    C --> E[Ag2CrO4 Brick Red Precipitate with CrO4 2 minus]
    D --> F{Equivalence Point Reached?}
    F -- Not Yet --> G[Pathway 1 Dominant Lower Ksp]
    F -- Yes --> H[Pathway 2 Triggers Endpoint Signal]
    G --> A
    H --> I[Persistent Brick Red Color Marks End]
```

## 4.3 Precipitation Sequence Hierarchy Diagram

This block diagram explains **why AgCl precipitates before Ag₂CrO₄**, anchoring the rationale in the **Ksp-based solubility hierarchy**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[AgNO3 Added Dropwise] --> B0[Ag plus Ions Released in Solution]
    B0 --> C0{Which Anion to Precipitate With?}
    C0 --> D0[Cl minus Ksp 1.76 times 10 power minus 10]
    C0 --> E0[CrO4 2 minus Ksp 1.10 times 10 power minus 12]
    D0 --> F0[AgCl Forms First Lower Solubility Wins]
    E0 --> G0[Ag2CrO4 Forms Only After Cl minus Depleted]
    F0 --> H0[White Curdy Precipitate Observed]
    G0 --> I0[Brick Red Endpoint Signal]
    H0 --> J0[Continue Titration]
    I0 --> K0[Stop Titration Note Burette Reading]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for Sample pH Adjustment

A frequently asked question in KTU vivas is: *"What if the sample pH is not in the 7–8.5 range?"* The following decision tree resolves this.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Measure Initial Sample pH] --> P1{pH less than 6.5?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2[Add NaHCO3 Powder to Neutralize]
    P1 -- No --> P3{pH between 7.0 and 8.5?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Proceed with Titration]
    P3 -- No --> P5{pH greater than 10?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Add Dilute HNO3 to Bring to 8.0]
    P5 -- No --> P4
    P2 --> P3
    P6 --> P4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define chloride. Mention the principle of Mohr's method for the determination of chloride in water.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Chloride is the negatively charged halogen ion (Cl⁻) commonly found in natural waters, originating from salt dissolution, seawater intrusion, and industrial discharges.
- **Principle (2 marks):** Mohr's method is a precipitation titration in which chloride ions in the water sample are titrated against a standard silver nitrate (AgNO₃) solution using **potassium chromate (K₂CrO₄) as an indicator**. Silver chloride (AgCl, white) precipitates first due to its lower Ksp. At the equivalence point, the excess Ag⁺ reacts with chromate to form a brick-red Ag₂CrO₄ precipitate, marking the endpoint.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is K₂CrO₄ used as the indicator in Mohr's method? Why is the pH maintained between 7 and 8.5?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **K₂CrO₄ as indicator (1.5 marks):** Potassium chromate is used because silver chromate (Ag₂CrO₄) has a distinctive **brick-red color** that is visually distinct from the white AgCl precipitate, providing a sharp endpoint. Its Ksp is *higher* than AgCl, ensuring it precipitates only after all Cl⁻ is consumed.
- **pH range 7–8.5 (1.5 marks):** In acidic conditions (pH < 6.5), CrO₄²⁻ converts to Cr₂O₇²⁻ (dichromate), weakening the indicator. In strongly alkaline solutions (pH > 10), Ag⁺ forms brown Ag₂O, causing premature endpoint. Therefore, the narrow pH window of 7.0–8.5 is critical for accuracy.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the principle, reactions, and indicator chemistry involved in the determination of chloride by Mohr's method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the stepwise procedure for the determination of chloride in a water sample and explain the significance of the blank titration. A 50 mL water sample required 12.40 mL of 0.0141 N AgNO₃. The blank reading was 0.20 mL. Calculate the chloride concentration and comment on its suitability for drinking as per IS 10500:2012. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Principle (2 marks):** Mohr's method is an argentometric precipitation titration. Chloride ions react quantitatively with Ag⁺ from AgNO₃ to form insoluble AgCl. The endpoint is detected using K₂CrO₄ indicator, which forms a brick-red Ag₂CrO₄ precipitate at the equivalence point.
- **Primary reaction (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{Ag}^+_{(aq)} + \text{Cl}^-_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} \downarrow \;\; (\text{white}) $$

- **Indicator (endpoint) reaction (1.5 marks):**

$$ 2\text{Ag}^+_{(aq)} + \text{CrO}_4^{2-}_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 \downarrow \;\; (\text{brick red}) $$

- **Indicator chemistry and pH (2 marks):** The Ksp of AgCl (1.76 × 10⁻¹⁰) is lower than that of Ag₂CrO₄ (1.10 × 10⁻¹²; *effective solubility governs the order*), so AgCl precipitates preferentially. At pH 7.0–8.5, chromate remains as CrO₄²⁻ and silver is stable, ensuring a sharp endpoint. Outside this pH range, dichromate formation or Ag₂O precipitation causes errors.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Procedure (2 marks):**
  1. Pipette 50 mL of water sample into a clean conical flask.
  2. Add 1 mL of 5% K₂CrO₄ indicator → solution turns lemon yellow.
  3. Titrate against 0.0141 N AgNO₃ from the burette with constant swirling.
  4. Endpoint: first appearance of persistent brick-red color (≥ 30 seconds).
  5. Record titre value (V_s = 12.40 mL).
  6. Repeat with distilled water blank (V_b = 0.20 mL).

- **Significance of blank (1 mark):** The blank titration corrects for any chloride or interfering substances in the distilled water and indicator, ensuring the result reflects only the sample's chloride content.

- **Calculation (3 marks):**

$$ V_{\text{corrected}} = 12.40 - 0.20 = 12.20 \; \text{mL} $$

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{12.20 \times 0.0141 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{50} $$

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{12.20 \times 0.0141}{1} = 0.17202 $$

$$ 0.17202 \times 35.45 = 6.098 \; (\text{per 50 mL basis in mg-eq units}) $$

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{6.098 \times 1000}{50} = \dfrac{6098}{50} = 121.96 \; \text{mg/L} $$

- **Comment (1 mark):** Since 121.96 mg/L < 250 mg/L (IS 10500:2012 acceptable limit), the water is **suitable for drinking** with respect to chloride content.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of neat chemical equations, explain the role of K₂CrO₄ as an indicator in Mohr's method. Discuss the effect of pH on the titration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater sample (100 mL) was titrated with 0.1 N AgNO₃ using K₂CrO₄ indicator. The burette readings were: initial = 1.20 mL, final = 18.50 mL. Blank consumed 0.10 mL. Calculate chloride in mg/L. If this wastewater is to be discharged into a surface water body, will it comply with CPCB effluent standards? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Role of K₂CrO₄ (3 marks):** K₂CrO₄ acts as a **visual precipitation indicator**. Since AgCl (Ksp = 1.76 × 10⁻¹⁰) is less soluble than Ag₂CrO₄ (Ksp = 1.10 × 10⁻¹² effective), AgCl precipitates first. When *all* Cl⁻ ions are consumed at the equivalence point, the next drop of Ag⁺ reacts with CrO₄²⁻ to form Ag₂CrO₄, signaling the endpoint by a **persistent brick-red color**.
- **Chemical equations (2 marks):**

$$ \text{Ag}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{AgCl} \; (\text{white, primary}) $$

$$ 2\text{Ag}^+ + \text{CrO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{Ag}_2\text{CrO}_4 \; (\text{brick red, endpoint}) $$

- **Effect of pH (2 marks):** At pH < 6.5, CrO₄²⁻ + H⁺ → HCrO₄⁻ → Cr₂O₇²⁻, decreasing indicator concentration and delaying the endpoint. At pH > 10, 2Ag⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Ag₂O + H₂O (black-brown), causing *premature* endpoint. Hence, pH 7.0–8.5 is the optimal window.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Given data (1 mark):** V_sample = 100 mL; N = 0.1 N; V_initial = 1.20 mL; V_final = 18.50 mL; V_blank = 0.10 mL.

- **Sample titre (1 mark):**

$$ V_s = 18.50 - 1.20 = 17.30 \; \text{mL} $$

- **Blank correction (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{corrected}} = 17.30 - 0.10 = 17.20 \; \text{mL} $$

- **Calculation (3 marks):**

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{17.20 \times 0.1 \times 35.45 \times 1000}{100} $$

$$ 17.20 \times 0.1 = 1.72 $$

$$ 1.72 \times 35.45 = 60.974 $$

$$ 60.974 \times 1000 = 60974 $$

$$ \text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{60974}{100} = 609.74 \; \text{mg/L} $$

- **Compliance comment (1 mark):** As per **CPCB effluent discharge standards (General Standards for Inland Surface Water — Schedule VI, The Environment (Protection) Rules, 1986)**, chloride is not a strictly regulated parameter; however, BIS 10500 recommends ≤ 250 mg/L for surface water sources. The calculated value of **609.74 mg/L** exceeds this limit, indicating the wastewater **does not comply** with desirable surface water quality and would require **pretreatment (ion exchange, RO, or electrodialysis)** before discharge.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Blank subtraction omission (–2 marks):** Many students forget to subtract the blank titre. Always perform and report a blank titration.
> 2. **Wrong normality used (–2 marks):** The normality of AgNO₃ must be the one *actually* used in the burette (0.0141 N for drinking water or 0.1 N for wastewater), not the theoretical value.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing mL and L without proper conversion factors leads to wrong answers. Always re-check that the 1000 factor is appropriately placed.
> 4. **Forgetting the standard formula (–2 marks):** Memorize the formula: $\text{Cl}^- = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$.
> 5. **Not commenting on IS/CPCB limits (–1 mark):** The "interpretation" or "comment" sub-part carries 1–2 marks. Always relate the calculated value to the relevant standard (IS 10500:2012 or CPCB).
> 6. **Using NaOH instead of NaHCO₃ for pH adjustment (–1 mark):** NaOH can overshoot alkalinity, causing Ag₂O precipitation. NaHCO₃ is a milder buffer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Method name:** Mohr's method (Argentometric precipitation titration)
- ☐ **APHA reference:** Method 4500-Cl⁻ B
- ☐ **Titrant:** Standard AgNO₃ (commonly 0.0141 N)
- ☐ **Indicator:** K₂CrO₄ (5% w/v, 1 mL dose)
- ☐ **Endpoint color:** White → pale yellow → **persistent brick-red**
- ☐ **Reactions to memorize:** Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ → AgCl (white); 2Ag⁺ + CrO₄²⁻ → Ag₂CrO₄ (brick red)
- ☐ **Equivalent weight of Cl⁻:** **35.45 g/eq** (non-negotiable in KTU exams)
- ☐ **Master formula:** $\text{Cl}^- \; (\text{mg/L}) = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \times N \times 35.45 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$
- ☐ **Always perform a blank titration** and subtract the volume
- ☐ **pH window:** **7.0 to 8.5** (use NaHCO₃, not NaOH, to adjust)
- ☐ **Interfering ions:** Br⁻, I⁻, S²⁻, CN⁻, PO₄³⁻, colored/turbid samples
- ☐ **Endpoint persistence:** Color must last **≥ 30 seconds**
- ☐ **Sample volume:** 50 mL (drinking water) or 100 mL (wastewater)
- ☐ **Dilute high-strength samples** if Cl⁻ > 500 mg/L
- ☐ **BIS 10500:2012 limits:** Acceptable = 250 mg/L; Permissible = 1000 mg/L
- ☐ **AgNO₃ storage:** Store in **amber bottles** away from sunlight (AgNO₃ is photosensitive)
- ☐ **Washing the burette:** Rinse with the AgNO₃ solution *before* filling (not with distilled water)
- ☐ **Reading convention:** Upper meniscus for AgNO₃; lower meniscus for colored solutions
- ☐ **Report units:** Always report in **mg/L** (or ppm, equivalent for dilute aqueous solutions)
- ☐ **Significance:** Cl⁻ > 250 mg/L → corrosive to metals; > 500 mg/L → salty taste and saline soil issues
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of pH, Electrical Conductivity and Turbidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of pH, Electrical Conductivity and Turbidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Water Quality Parameters

Water quality assessment relies on a triad of fundamental physical-chemical parameters that govern the potability, aesthetic acceptability, and ecological suitability of water. **pH**, **Electrical Conductivity (EC)**, and **Turbidity** constitute the most frequently measured indices in any environmental quality analysis laboratory because they provide rapid, inexpensive, and reliable first-line diagnostics of water contamination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCVL508)**
> As per the KTU 2024 B.Tech (Civil Engineering) scheme, this experiment is a **compulsory core lab experiment** under Module 2 of the Environmental Quality Analysis Lab. It is mapped to **CO2**: *Apply standard analytical methods to characterize water and wastewater samples* and is tested in the **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** viva-voce and practical record assessment.

---

## 1.2 Definition of pH

The formal academic definition of pH, as adopted by the **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC)** and endorsed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS IS 3025:Part 11)**, is:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10} \left[ \text{H}^{+} \right] \quad \text{(activity of hydrogen ions, dimensionless)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **pH is a logarithmic measure of the hydrogen ion activity in an aqueous solution, ranging from 0 (highly acidic) to 14 (highly alkaline), with 7 representing absolute neutrality at 25 °C.**

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine pH as a **"measure of sourness or soapiness"** in water. Pure water is neutral (pH = 7), like room-temperature freshwater. Add lemon juice (citric acid) and it becomes sour — pH drops below 7. Add baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) and the water feels slippery and soapy — pH rises above 7. A logarithmic pH means each unit change is **10 times more acidic or alkaline** than the previous one. So a water sample at pH 5 is **10 times more acidic** than pH 6 and **100 times more acidic** than pH 7. The **WHO and BIS IS 10500:2012** drinking water standards mandate a permissible pH range of **6.5 to 8.5**.

---

## 1.3 Definition of Electrical Conductivity (EC)

Electrical conductivity is the quantitative measure of a water sample's ability to transmit an electric current, which is directly proportional to the concentration of dissolved ionizable solids (chiefly salts of sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, chloride, sulfate, and bicarbonate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Conductivity (EC or κ, kappa) is the reciprocal of resistivity (ρ), expressed in Siemens per centimetre (S/cm) or, more commonly for environmental samples, in microSiemens per centimetre (µS/cm) or milliSiemens per centimetre (mS/cm).**

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of water as a **"highway for tiny charged particles (ions)"**. Pure distilled water is an empty highway — almost no cars can travel on it, so its conductivity is very low (~0.055 µS/cm). When salts dissolve, they release positively charged ions (cations like Na⁺, Ca²⁺) and negatively charged ions (anions like Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻), which act like cars moving along the highway under an applied voltage. The more cars (ions), the more current flows, and the higher the conductivity. A salty seawater sample at 50,000 µS/cm is essentially a fully packed rush-hour highway, while distilled water is an empty road at midnight.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Turbidity

Turbidity is an **optical property** of water that quantifies the degree to which light is scattered and absorbed by suspended particulate matter (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms, and colloids) rather than transmitted in a straight line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turbidity is measured in Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU) using a nephelometer that detects light scattered at 90° to the incident beam, in accordance with IS 3025:Part 10 and the standard APHA 2130 method.**

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine **shining a flashlight into a swimming pool at night**. In a perfectly clean pool, the beam is invisible because light travels straight through. In a murky, muddy pool, the beam becomes a glowing cone of scattered light — that visibility of the light beam is **turbidity**. The murkier the water, the more suspended particles bounce the light around, and the more turbid the water appears. **WHO drinking water guidelines stipulate turbidity should be < 1 NTU**, with a maximum permissible limit of **5 NTU** (BIS IS 10500:2012) for aesthetically acceptable drinking water.

---

## 1.5 Standards & Regulatory Limits (Quick Reference)

| Parameter | Unit | IS 10500:2012 (Drinking Water) | WHO Guidelines | Instrument Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **pH** | dimensionless | **6.5 – 8.5** | 6.5 – 8.5 | Digital pH meter |
| **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** | µS/cm | — | — | Conductivity meter |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | mg/L | 500 (max 2000) | 1000 | Calculated from EC |
| **Turbidity** | NTU | **1 (max 5)** | < 1 (ideally) | Nephelometric turbidimeter |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship **TDS (mg/L) ≈ 0.55 × EC (µS/cm)** is widely used for freshwater samples at 25 °C, with conversion factors ranging from **0.5 to 0.7** depending on ionic composition (USEPA Method 120.1 reference).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of pH Measurement

### 2.1.1 The pH Scale and Nernst Equation

The pH electrode assembly consists of two half-cells:
1. A **glass electrode** (sensing half-cell) — develops a potential proportional to the H⁺ activity of the sample.
2. A **reference electrode** (typically a Saturated Calomel Electrode, SCE, or Ag/AgCl) — provides a constant, stable reference potential.

The cell EMF (E_cell) follows the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, R \, T}{F} \times \text{pH}$$

where:
- $E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ}$ = standard cell potential (constant)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\frac{2.303 \, R \, T}{F}$ = Nernst slope = **0.0591 V per pH unit at 25 °C (298.15 K)**

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}}$$

### 2.1.2 Why Calibration is Essential

Because $E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ}$ drifts with electrode aging, junction potential, and liquid-liquid boundary effects, **two-point calibration** is performed using buffer solutions of known pH (typically pH 4.0, 7.0, and 9.2). The instrument internally computes the slope and intercept.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Electrical Conductivity Measurement

### 2.2.1 Conductance and Ohm's Law

By **Ohm's Law**, the resistance R (ohms, Ω) of an electrolyte solution is:

$$R = \frac{\rho \, L}{A} = \frac{L}{\kappa \, A}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·cm)
- $\kappa$ = conductivity (S/cm) — what we want
- $L$ = distance between electrodes (cm)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of electrolyte between electrodes (cm²)

Conductance $G$ is the reciprocal of resistance:

$$G = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{\kappa \, A}{L}$$

### 2.2.2 Cell Constant

The ratio $L/A$ is a geometric property of the conductivity cell, called the **cell constant (K_cell)**:

$$\boxed{\kappa = G \times K_{\text{cell}} = \frac{K_{\text{cell}}}{R}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cell constant is **experimentally determined** by measuring the resistance of a standard KCl solution of known conductivity (e.g., 0.01 M KCl gives 1413 µS/cm at 25 °C). Typical dip-type conductivity cells have $K_{\text{cell}} \approx 0.1$ to **1.5 cm⁻¹**.

### 2.2.3 Temperature Dependence

EC increases by approximately **2% per °C** rise in temperature. All measurements must therefore be **temperature-compensated to 25 °C (reference temperature)**. Modern meters use either linear (2%/°C) or non-linear (standard ISO 7888) temperature correction algorithms.

### 2.2.4 EC to TDS Conversion

The **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** is empirically related to EC by:

$$\boxed{\text{TDS (mg/L)} = K_{\text{conv}} \times \text{EC (µS/cm)}}$$

where $K_{\text{conv}}$ is a conversion factor (typically **0.55 to 0.7**; USEPA adopts 0.65 for brackish waters; for typical surface/groundwater, **0.55** is standard in KTU lab manuals).

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Basis of Turbidity Measurement

### 2.3.1 Nephelometric Principle

A **nephelometer** measures the intensity of light scattered at a **90° angle** to the incident beam. The intensity of scattered light follows the **Rayleigh scattering law** for small particles (diameter < 1/10 of wavelength):

$$I_{\text{scattered}} \propto \frac{N \cdot V^2}{\lambda^4} \cdot I_0$$

where:
- $N$ = number of particles per unit volume
- $V$ = volume of a single particle
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light

This proportionality (linear in particle concentration) is why turbidity readings are linear over a wide operational range.

### 2.3.2 Formazin Primary Standard

**Formazin polymer** (a condensate of hydrazine sulfate and hexamethylenetetramine) is the **primary reference turbidity standard**:
- **4000 NTU** stock suspension is prepared by mixing stoichiometric amounts of reagents.
- Working standards (0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000 NTU) are obtained by **serial dilution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not Jackson Candle Turbidimeter anymore?** The older JTU method (Jackson Candle) is limited to 25–1000 NTU, has poor reproducibility at low values, and is no longer recommended. The **nephelometric NTU method** has a range of **0–4000 NTU** and is the **IS 3025:Part 10 / APHA 2130 B** standard.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^{+}]$ | Definition of pH | dimensionless | 25 °C reference; activity, not concentration |
| 2 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ | Nernst equation (combined electrode) | Volts (V) | Valid at 25 °C; 0.0591 V/pH slope |
| 3 | $K_{\text{cell}} = \kappa_{\text{std}} \times R_{\text{std}}$ | Cell constant determination | cm⁻¹ | Standard: 0.01 M KCl, 1413 µS/cm at 25 °C |
| 4 | $\kappa = \dfrac{K_{\text{cell}}}{R_{\text{sample}}}$ | Sample conductivity | µS/cm or mS/cm | Measured resistance R in Ω |
| 5 | $\text{TDS} = 0.55 \times \text{EC}$ | TDS from EC | mg/L | For typical freshwater; range 0.5–0.7 |
| 6 | $\text{EC}_{25} = \dfrac{\text{EC}_T}{1 + 0.02(T - 25)}$ | Linear temp correction | µS/cm | T in °C; 2% per °C |
| 7 | $C_{\text{working}} = C_{\text{stock}} \times \dfrac{V_{\text{aliquot}}}{V_{\text{final}}}$ | Standard dilution (formazin) | NTU | E.g., 1 mL of 4000 NTU in 100 mL → 40 NTU |
| 8 | $\text{Decimal dilution factor} = 10^{-(\text{dilution})}$ | Serial dilution shortcut | dimensionless | e.g., 10⁻² dilution = 1:100 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Parameter | Practical Importance |
|---|---|
| **pH** | Controls corrosivity of distribution pipes; governs disinfection efficiency of chlorine; influences aquatic ecosystem (fish survive in 6.5–8.5); critical in coagulation–flocculation in water treatment plants. |
| **EC** | Rapid surrogate for TDS; used in irrigation classification (USDA: < 250 µS/cm low salinity hazard); detects saline intrusion in coastal aquifers; online monitoring of industrial effluents. |
| **Turbidity** | Shields pathogens from UV/ozone disinfection; high turbidity in surface water signals runoff and contamination; measured continuously in WTP filter effluents; correlated with TSS for process control. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Slope at 25 °C

Starting from the general Nernst equation for the half-cell reaction $\text{H}^{+} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

Step 1 — For the H⁺/H₂ couple with n = 1 and $E^{\circ} = 0$ V (Standard Hydrogen Electrode, SHE):

$$E = -\frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{1}{[\text{H}^{+}]} = +\frac{RT}{F} \ln [\text{H}^{+}]$$

Step 2 — Converting to log₁₀ using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log_{10} [\text{H}^{+}]$$

Step 3 — Substituting pH = $-\log_{10}[\text{H}^{+}]$, hence $\log_{10}[\text{H}^{+}] = -\text{pH}$:

$$E = -\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \times \text{pH}$$

Step 4 — Evaluating the constant at T = 25 °C = 298.15 K:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

Step 5 — Final compact form (glass electrode + reference combined):

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}_{\text{sample}}}$$

> This slope (**59.16 mV per pH unit**) is the cornerstone of all electrometric pH measurements and must be **verified** during two-point calibration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cell Constant from Standard KCl

A 0.01 M KCl solution has a **standard conductivity of 1413 µS/cm at 25 °C** (IS 3025:Part 14 / ISO 7888). When this standard solution is placed in a conductivity cell, the cell constant is determined by:

Step 1 — Ohm's Law applied to the KCl standard:

$$R_{\text{std}} = \frac{1}{G_{\text{std}}} = \frac{1}{\kappa_{\text{std}}} \times \frac{L}{A}$$

Step 2 — The cell constant is defined as the geometric ratio:

$$K_{\text{cell}} = \frac{L}{A} \quad (\text{units: cm}^{-1})$$

Step 3 — Combining the two expressions:

$$K_{\text{cell}} = \kappa_{\text{std}} \times R_{\text{std}}$$

Step 4 — Substituting numerical values (Example: if measured R_std = 707 Ω for 0.01 M KCl):

$$K_{\text{cell}} = 1413 \, \mu\text{S/cm} \times 707 \, \Omega = 999{,}000 \, \mu\text{S} \cdot \Omega / \text{cm}$$

Step 5 — Since $1 \, \mu\text{S} \times 1 \, \Omega = 10^{-6}$ (dimensionless), the unit simplifies to **cm⁻¹**:

$$K_{\text{cell}} = 999{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{cm}^{-1} = 0.999 \, \text{cm}^{-1} \approx 1.0 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **0.999 cm⁻¹** is a very common value for laboratory dip-cells and should be cross-checked in the KTU lab manual for the assigned instrument.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TDS from EC (USEPA Approximation)

Step 1 — The total dissolved solids in mg/L can be expressed as:

$$\text{TDS} = \sum_i c_i \cdot M_i$$

where $c_i$ is the molar concentration and $M_i$ the molar mass of each dissolved ionic species.

Step 2 — For typical freshwater dominated by Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, HCO₃⁻, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, an empirical linear regression of hundreds of measured samples gave:

$$\text{TDS} \approx 0.55 \times \text{EC}_{25}$$

Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example: If measured EC = 845 µS/cm at 25 °C:

$$\text{TDS} = 0.55 \times 845 = 464.75 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 465 \, \text{mg/L}$$

Step 4 — This lies within the BIS IS 10500:2012 permissible limit of 500 mg/L, indicating the water is **potable with respect to TDS**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Standard Formazin Dilution Scheme

Step 1 — The 4000 NTU stock is prepared by:
- Dissolving **5.0 g hydrazine sulfate** in 400 mL distilled water.
- Dissolving **50.0 g hexamethylenetetramine** in 400 mL distilled water.
- Mixing the two, diluting to 1 L, and allowing to stand 48 h at 25 °C.

Step 2 — To prepare **100 mL of 40 NTU** working standard:

$$C_{\text{working}} = C_{\text{stock}} \times \frac{V_{\text{stock}}}{V_{\text{final}}}$$

Step 3 — Solving for the required volume of stock:

$$V_{\text{stock}} = \frac{C_{\text{working}} \times V_{\text{final}}}{C_{\text{stock}}} = \frac{40 \times 100}{4000} = 1.0 \, \text{mL}$$

Step 4 — Therefore, **pipette 1.0 mL of 4000 NTU stock** into a 100 mL volumetric flask and make up to the mark with particle-free (0.2 µm filtered) distilled water to obtain 40 NTU.

Step 5 — Other standards prepared similarly (e.g., 10 NTU → 0.25 mL stock; 100 NTU → 2.5 mL stock).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Summary

### 3.5.1 Determination of pH (IS 3025:Part 11, Electrometric Method)

1. Switch on the pH meter; allow **30 minutes warm-up**.
2. Rinse the combined glass electrode with distilled water; gently blot dry with tissue (do **not rub**).
3. Immerse in **pH 7.0 buffer**; adjust the **CALIBRATE/SLOPE** knob to read 7.00.
4. Rinse, blot, immerse in **pH 4.0 buffer**; adjust the **SLOPE** knob to read 4.00.
5. Repeat until both readings are stable within ±0.02 pH units.
6. (Optional) Verify with **pH 9.2 buffer** for alkaline range.
7. Rinse, blot, immerse in sample; record reading when stable (typically 30–60 s).
8. Report pH to **two decimal places** with sample temperature noted.

### 3.5.2 Determination of EC (IS 3025:Part 14)

1. Verify the cell constant daily using **0.01 M KCl** (1413 µS/cm at 25 °C).
2. Rinse the conductivity cell three times with the sample (rinsing = discard, do not return to bulk).
3. Immerse cell in sample; ensure electrodes fully submerged and free of air bubbles (tap gently to dislodge).
4. Allow reading to stabilize; record conductivity, temperature, and TDS (if displayed).
5. For high-EC samples (> 2000 µS/cm), use a cell with lower K_cell or dilute the sample with known EC distilled water and re-measure.

### 3.5.3 Determination of Turbidity (IS 3025:Part 10, Nephelometric)

1. **Calibrate** the nephelometer using formazin standards spanning the expected range (e.g., 0, 10, 100, 1000 NTU).
2. Use **clean, optically clear sample vials** (matched borosilicate tubes); rinse thrice with sample.
3. **Wipe the outside of the vial** with a soft lint-free tissue — fingerprints and smudges scatter light and cause reading errors.
4. Handle vials by the **cap or upper rim** only (avoid finger contact on optical surfaces).
5. Insert the vial into the sample chamber; close the lid; allow air bubbles to settle (wait 30 s).
6. Read NTU directly from the digital display. **Record triplicate readings**; report the mean.
7. If reading exceeds range, dilute the sample and multiply by dilution factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Decanting vs. Pipetting:** Turbidity is highly sensitive to **mixing-induced resuspension**. Always gently invert the sample bottle 2–3 times; **never shake vigorously**. Vigorous shaking introduces microbubbles that read as turbidity.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Numerical

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023, Modified]:**

A water sample was analysed in the lab. The following data were obtained:
- Resistance of 0.01 M KCl at 25 °C = 720 Ω
- Resistance of water sample at 25 °C = 1450 Ω
- pH of sample (from pH meter) = 7.42
- Turbidity of sample = 18.6 NTU

**Calculate:**
(a) The cell constant of the conductivity cell
(b) The electrical conductivity of the sample
(c) The approximate TDS of the sample
(d) Comment on potability of the water with reference to IS 10500:2012.

**Solution:**

(a) Cell constant:

$$K_{\text{cell}} = \kappa_{\text{KCl}} \times R_{\text{KCl}} = 1413 \, \mu\text{S/cm} \times 720 \, \Omega$$

Converting: $1413 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{S/cm} \times 720 \, \Omega$

$$K_{\text{cell}} = 1413 \times 720 \times 10^{-6} = 1{,}017{,}360 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{cm}^{-1} = 1.0174 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

(b) Sample conductivity:

$$\kappa_{\text{sample}} = \frac{K_{\text{cell}}}{R_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{1.0174 \, \text{cm}^{-1}}{1450 \, \Omega} = 0.0007017 \, \text{S/cm} = 701.7 \, \mu\text{S/cm}$$

(c) Approximate TDS:

$$\text{TDS} = 0.55 \times \kappa_{\text{sample}} = 0.55 \times 701.7 = 385.9 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 386 \, \text{mg/L}$$

(d) Potability comment:
- pH = 7.42 → within 6.5–8.5 → **acceptable** ✓
- TDS = 386 mg/L → within 500 mg/L → **acceptable** ✓
- Turbidity = 18.6 NTU → exceeds 1 NTU (and even 5 NTU max limit) → **NOT acceptable** ✗

**Conclusion:** The water sample is fit for drinking with respect to pH and TDS but **fails the turbidity criterion**, indicating the need for filtration/settling before supply.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define pH. Mention the BIS IS 10500:2012 permissible range for drinking water.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- pH is defined as the negative logarithm (base 10) of the hydrogen ion activity in an aqueous solution: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺] (1 mark)
- It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the acidic or alkaline nature of water on a scale of 0–14 (1 mark)
- As per BIS IS 10500:2012, the acceptable range of pH for drinking water is **6.5 to 8.5** (1 mark)

### Q2. What is the difference between NTU and JTU?
**[KTU University Exam - Jul 2022 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit)** is measured using a nephelometer, which detects light scattered at **90°** to the incident beam (1.5 marks)
- **JTU (Jackson Turbidity Unit)** is measured using a Jackson Candle Turbidimeter, a historical method based on the visual disappearance of a candle flame through a water column (1 mark)
- The NTU method is more sensitive, has a wider range (0–4000 NTU), and is the **current standard (IS 3025:Part 10)**, whereas JTU is largely obsolete (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply / Analyse]**

(a) **Describe the principle, working, and procedure for the determination of pH of a water sample using a digital pH meter. Draw a neat labeled diagram of the electrode system.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Principle (2 marks):*
The electrometric pH measurement is based on the **Nernst Equation**. A glass electrode develops a potential proportional to the H⁺ activity of the sample, while a reference electrode (saturated calomel) maintains a constant potential. The cell EMF is linearly related to pH with a slope of **59.16 mV per pH unit at 25 °C**:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - 0.0591 \times \text{pH}$$

*Working (2 marks):*
The pH meter uses a **high-impedance amplifier** (to prevent current flow that would polarize the electrodes), an ADC, and a microprocessor that applies the Nernst slope to compute and display pH digitally. Temperature is automatically sensed and the slope corrected.

*Procedure (2 marks):*
1. Switch on meter, warm up 30 min.
2. Two-point calibration with pH 4.0 and pH 7.0 buffers; adjust CAL and SLOPE knobs.
3. Rinse electrode with distilled water, blot dry.
4. Immerse in sample, stir gently, read when stable.
5. Report pH to two decimal places at the recorded temperature.

*Diagram (1 mark):* Labeled sketch showing glass bulb, inner reference, KCl filling, reference electrode, salt bridge, high-impedance meter, and digital display.

(b) **The EMF of a glass-calomel electrode system was 0.175 V in a pH 4.01 buffer and 0.351 V in an unknown water sample at 25 °C. Calculate the pH of the sample.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Use the linear Nernst relationship (1 mark):*
$$\text{pH} = \text{pH}_{\text{buffer}} + \frac{E_{\text{buffer}} - E_{\text{sample}}}{0.0591}$$

*Step 2 — Substitute the given values (2 marks):*
$$\text{pH} = 4.01 + \frac{0.175 - 0.351}{0.0591} = 4.01 + \frac{-0.176}{0.0591}$$

*Step 3 — Perform the division (2 marks):*
$$\frac{-0.176}{0.0591} = -2.978$$

*Step 4 — Compute the final pH (1 mark):*
$$\text{pH} = 4.01 - 2.978 = 1.032 \approx 1.03$$

*Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):*
A pH of 1.03 is **strongly acidic** and **far below the BIS 10500:2012 limit of 6.5–8.5**, indicating severe industrial/chemical contamination; the water is **not potable**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply / Analyse]**

(a) **Explain the principle, apparatus, and procedure for the determination of turbidity of a water sample using a nephelometric turbidimeter.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Principle (2 marks):*
Turbidity is determined by measuring the intensity of light **scattered at 90°** to an incident light beam by suspended particles. The intensity is proportional to particle concentration and follows the **Rayleigh scattering equation** for small particles. Results are expressed in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.

*Apparatus (2 marks):*
- Nephelometric turbidimeter (tungsten lamp, photodetector at 90°, matched sample vials)
- Formazin primary standard (4000 NTU stock)
- Volumetric flasks, pipettes, particle-free distilled water
- Sample bottles (amber-coloured, acid-washed)

*Procedure (2 marks):*
1. Prepare formazin working standards (e.g., 10, 100, 1000 NTU) by serial dilution.
2. Calibrate the instrument using these standards.
3. Rinse the sample vial thrice with sample, fill to mark, cap.
4. Wipe the outside with lint-free tissue; handle by the cap.
5. Insert into sample chamber, close lid, wait 30 s for bubble settling.
6. Read NTU directly; record triplicate readings and report the mean.

*Precautions (1 mark):* No air bubbles, no fingerprints, no vigorous shaking, calibrate daily.

(b) **The electrical conductivity of a water sample was measured using a dip-type conductivity cell. The resistance of 0.01 M KCl (standard = 1413 µS/cm at 25 °C) was 735 Ω, and the resistance of the sample at 25 °C was 1820 Ω. Calculate (i) the cell constant, (ii) the conductivity of the sample, and (iii) the approximate TDS. Comment on the potability with respect to IS 10500:2012.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Cell constant (2 marks):*
$$K_{\text{cell}} = \kappa_{\text{KCl}} \times R_{\text{KCl}} = 1413 \, \mu\text{S/cm} \times 735 \, \Omega$$
$$K_{\text{cell}} = 1413 \times 10^{-6} \times 735 = 1{,}038{,}555 \times 10^{-6} = 1.039 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

*Step 2 — Sample conductivity (2 marks):*
$$\kappa_{\text{sample}} = \frac{K_{\text{cell}}}{R_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{1.039 \, \text{cm}^{-1}}{1820 \, \Omega} = 0.0005709 \, \text{S/cm} = 570.9 \, \mu\text{S/cm} \approx 571 \, \mu\text{S/cm}$$

*Step 3 — Approximate TDS (1.5 marks):*
$$\text{TDS} = 0.55 \times \kappa_{\text{sample}} = 0.55 \times 571 = 314.05 \, \text{mg/L} \approx 314 \, \text{mg/L}$$

*Step 4 — Potability comment (1.5 marks):*
- TDS = 314 mg/L is **less than 500 mg/L** → within BIS 10500:2012 permissible limit → **potable with respect to TDS** ✓
- However, EC alone is not a complete potability criterion; pH, turbidity, microbial, and toxic metal analyses must also be performed before final acceptance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
> 1. **Forgetting the cell constant dimension** — Students frequently write $K_{\text{cell}}$ in Ω or S without the cm⁻¹ unit. Always state the unit as **cm⁻¹** explicitly (loses 0.5–1 mark).
> 2. **Using molarity instead of activity in pH** — pH uses *activity*, but for dilute solutions (< 0.1 M) the approximation [H⁺] ≈ activity is acceptable. Writing "pH = −log[H⁺] in mol/L" is fine but mark the activity basis.
> 3. **Confusing NTU with mg/L** — Turbidity (NTU) is an *optical* property; TSS (mg/L) is a *gravimetric* property. They are correlated but NOT the same. Do not report turbidity in mg/L.
> 4. **No temperature correction in EC** — Examiners will deduct marks if you report EC without stating the temperature. The standard reference is **25 °C**.
> 5. **Forgetting the slope at non-25 °C** — At T = 30 °C, slope = 0.0601 V/pH; at T = 20 °C, slope = 0.0582 V/pH. Use 0.0591 only at 25 °C.
> 6. **Reporting pH with more than 2 decimals** — pH meters display 2 decimals; reporting 7.423 is meaningless and loses 0.5 mark for "lack of significant figures awareness".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — 1-Page Summary**

### Core Definitions
- **pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]**; range 0–14; BIS 10500 limit 6.5–8.5.
- **EC (κ)** = Reciprocal of resistivity; S/cm or µS/cm; reference temp 25 °C.
- **TDS (mg/L) ≈ 0.55 × EC (µS/cm)** for typical freshwater.
- **Turbidity** = Light scattering at 90°; unit NTU; BIS 10500 limit 1 NTU (max 5 NTU).

### Key Numerical Constants
- **Nernst slope at 25 °C** = **0.0591 V per pH unit**.
- **0.01 M KCl conductivity at 25 °C** = **1413 µS/cm**.
- **TDS conversion factor** = **0.55** (range 0.5–0.7).
- **EC temperature coefficient** = **2% per °C**.
- **Formazin stock** = **4000 NTU**.

### Instruments & Their Key Parts
- **pH meter**: Combined glass + reference electrode; uses high-impedance amplifier; two-point calibration with pH 4 and 7.
- **Conductivity meter**: Platinum electrodes; AC signal (1 kHz) to prevent polarization; cell constant K ≈ 1 cm⁻¹.
- **Nephelometer**: Tungsten lamp; photodetector at 90°; formazin calibration; matched vials.

### Procedure Essentials
- Always **rinse** electrode/cell/vial three times with the sample.
- Always **blot dry** (pH) or **wipe** optical surface (turbidity) — never rub.
- Always **record temperature** alongside EC.
- Always report **triplicate mean ± standard deviation** for KTU lab records.
- Always check **air bubbles** before recording turbidity.

### Equations to Memorize (KTU Exam Essentials)
$$\text{pH} = \text{pH}_{\text{buffer}} + \frac{E_{\text{buffer}} - E_{\text{sample}}}{0.0591}$$
$$K_{\text{cell}} = \kappa_{\text{std}} \times R_{\text{std}}$$
$$\kappa_{\text{sample}} = \frac{K_{\text{cell}}}{R_{\text{sample}}}$$
$$\text{TDS} = 0.55 \times \kappa_{\text{sample}}$$
$$C_{\text{working}} = C_{\text{stock}} \times \frac{V_{\text{aliquot}}}{V_{\text{final}}}$$

### Quick Mnemonics
- **"pH-E-C-T"** for procedure order: **pH → EC → Conductivity → Turbidity** (always measure pH first, as it affects EC interpretation).
- **"No Bubbles, No Fingerprints, No Friction"** — the three golden rules of turbidity measurement.
- **"2-2-2"** for EC: **2 buffers for pH; 2 standards for EC (KCl + rinse); 2 decimal places** for pH reporting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Biological Oxygen Demand of Water and Wastewater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL508 - ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Biological Oxygen Demand of Water and Wastewater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO), expressed in **mg/L** or **ppm**, required by aerobic microorganisms to stabilize the decomposable organic matter present in water or wastewater under specified conditions of temperature and time, conventionally measured over **5 days at 20 °C** and denoted as **BOD₅** (Ref: APHA Standard Methods, 23rd Edition, Method 5210 B; IS 3025 Part 44:1993).

Mathematically expressed as the difference between the initial dissolved oxygen and the dissolved oxygen remaining after a stipulated incubation period, corrected for any seed contribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung Test" for Water

Imagine a polluted river as a **patient visiting a doctor**. The doctor measures how much oxygen the patient's lungs can absorb when asked to breathe deeply. Similarly, when we pour a water sample into a sealed bottle, we are asking the bacterial "patients" living in the water to *eat* the organic impurities. The rate at which these bacteria consume the dissolved oxygen (their "breathing rate") tells us exactly how much pollution is in the water.

- A clean mountain stream has a BOD of about **1–2 mg/L** (almost no breathlessness).
- A moderately polluted municipal sewage has BOD of about **200–400 mg/L** (heavy panting).
- Untreated industrial effluent can exceed **5,000–10,000 mg/L** (the patient is choking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 5 days at 20 °C?**
> In nature, rivers and streams generally have an average temperature of ~20 °C. The 5-day window captures the **carbonaceous biochemical oxidation** of organics, while ignoring the slower nitrification stage. This is the universal reference standard developed by the **Royal Commission on Sewage Disposal (UK, 1908)** and is still the global legal benchmark for organic pollution.

### 1.3 Salient Syllabus Highlights

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Test duration | **5 days** |
| Incubation temperature | **20 °C ± 1 °C** |
| Sample volume | **300 mL BOD bottle** |
| Reference standard | APHA 5210 B / IS 3025 Part 44 |
| Expression unit | **mg/L** |
| Typical municipal sewage BOD₅ | **200 – 400 mg/L** |
| CPCB inland surface water limit | **≤ 3.0 mg/L** |
| CPCB bathing water limit | **≤ 3.0 mg/L** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A question that simply asks "What is BOD?" expects the answer to include the **three modifiers** — (i) *biochemical* oxidation, (ii) *5 days* period, and (iii) *20 °C* temperature. Missing any one of these will cost you a mark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biochemical Foundation — The "Why" Behind BOD

The biochemical oxidation of organic carbon proceeds in two distinct stages:

1. **Carbonaceous Stage (Days 0–5):** Aerobic heterotrophic bacteria oxidize organic carbon compounds to **CO₂** and **H₂O**, consuming molecular O₂ in the process.

$$\text{Organic Matter (C, H, O, N)} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{enzymes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{biomass} + \text{energy}$$

2. **Nitrification Stage (Days 8–10+):** Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) oxidize ammonia to nitrate, consuming additional O₂.

$$2\text{NH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} 2\text{NO}_2^- + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

By terminating the test at 5 days, the BOD₅ result represents **only the carbonaceous demand**, which is the design parameter for biological treatment units.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — Stepwise Logic

The experimental workflow rests on the following sequential logic:

- **Step 1 — Sample Collection:** Grab sample in a BOD bottle ensuring no aeration; preserve at **4 °C** if analysis is delayed beyond 2 hours.
- **Step 2 — Dilution Justification:** Most wastewaters have BOD exceeding the saturation DO of water (~9.1 mg/L at 20 °C). The sample must be **diluted** with oxygen-saturated dilution water so that the resulting DO depletion is greater than **2 mg/L** and the residual DO is at least **1 mg/L** (quality control bounds).
- **Step 3 — Initial DO (D₁):** Measured on a portion of the diluted sample *immediately* after preparation using the **Winkler Iodometric Method** or a **membrane-electrode DO probe**.
- **Step 4 — Incubation:** The remaining BOD bottle is sealed (water-sealed stopper) and incubated in the dark for **5 days at 20 °C ± 1 °C** to prevent photosynthetic DO addition.
- **Step 5 — Final DO (D₂):** Measured on the incubated sample after exactly 5 days.
- **Step 6 — Seed Correction:** When the sample is suspected to have insufficient microbial population (e.g., industrial effluents with toxicants, chlorinated samples), a **seed** is added; a parallel seed-control bottle is run to subtract the oxygen demand of the seed itself.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$ | Unseeded dilution (no seed correction) | mg/L |
| 2 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$ | **Seeded dilution** with seed-control correction | mg/L |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{\text{Vol. of seed in diluted sample (mL)}}{\text{Vol. of seed in seed control (mL)}}$ | Seed ratio factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}}$ | Decimal volumetric fraction of sample | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{DO (Winkler)} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{sample}} \times N \times 8 \times 1000)}{V_{\text{bottle}}}$ | DO concentration from thiosulfate titration | mg/L |
| 6 | $\text{Dilution Factor} = \dfrac{1}{P}$ | Inverse of sample fraction | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} = \dfrac{\text{DO} \times V_{\text{bottle}}}{N \times 8000}$ | Rearranged titration formula (for reagent prep) | mL |

**Where:**
- $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample **before** incubation
- $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample **after** 5 days incubation
- $B_1$ = DO of seed control **before** incubation
- $B_2$ = DO of seed control **after** 5 days incubation
- $P$ = decimal fraction of sample in the dilution
- $f$ = seed correction factor
- $N$ = normality of sodium thiosulfate (typically 0.025 N)
- 8 = equivalent weight of oxygen (molecular weight 32 ÷ 4 electrons)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important sign convention:** $D_1$ must always be **greater** than $D_2$. If your titration gives $D_1 < D_2$, the result is invalid (bottle was contaminated, air entered, or you mixed up the labels) and must be rejected.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) Design:** Aeration tank volume and MLSS retention time are sized using $F/M$ ratio, which is anchored to the **influent BOD₅**.
- **Effluent Compliance:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) discharge norms mandate BOD₅ ≤ **30 mg/L** for inland disposal and ≤ **100 mg/L** for marine coastal disposal.
- **Self-Purification Studies:** Streeter–Phelps equation models the **DO sag curve** in a river downstream of a discharge point, with BOD₅ as the primary input parameter.
- **Industrial Effluent Toxicity Tracking:** A sudden drop in BOD removal efficiency signals either hydraulic overload, toxic shock, or loss of biomass in the aeration tank.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the BOD Formula (Seeded Case)

We start with the **fundamental mass balance principle** applied to the BOD bottle.

Let:
- $V$ = Volume of diluted sample in BOD bottle (300 mL)
- $V_s$ = Volume of original (un-diluted) sample in the bottle
- $V_d$ = Volume of dilution water in the bottle, with $V_s + V_d = V$
- $P = V_s / V$ (decimal fraction of sample)
- $V_{\text{seed,s}}$ = Volume of seed in the sample bottle
- $V_{\text{seed,c}}$ = Volume of seed in the seed-control bottle
- $f = V_{\text{seed,s}} / V_{\text{seed,c}}$ (seed ratio)

**Step 1 — Express oxygen consumed per litre of diluted sample:**

The mass of oxygen consumed by the organic matter in the sample portion of the bottle, per unit volume of diluted sample, is:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ consumed (mg/L diluted)} = D_1 - D_2$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the seed's own oxygen demand:**

The seed control bottle gives the **blank correction**. The oxygen consumed in the seed control is $B_1 - B_2$. The fraction of that blank attributable to the seed present in the sample bottle is:

$$\text{Seed correction} = (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f$$

**Step 3 — Net oxygen demand of the sample alone per litre of diluted water:**

$$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ of sample (per L diluted)} = (D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f$$

**Step 4 — Convert to oxygen demand per litre of *original* sample:**

The above value represents the demand exerted by only the $V_s$ portion of original sample. To express demand per litre of **original (undiluted) sample**, we divide by $P$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BOD}_5 \text{ (mg/L of original sample)} &= \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **seeded BOD formula** quoted in APHA Standard Methods.

**Step 5 — Simplification for the unseeded case:**

If no seed is added, then $B_1 = B_2$ (no oxygen consumption in the blank), and the equation collapses to:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** "Why is $P$ in the denominator and not the numerator?"
> **Answer:** Because we want the BOD *concentration* in the **original (un-diluted) sample**, not in the diluted mixture. Dividing by $P$ scales the measured demand back to the original concentration. This is analogous to the "back-titration scaling factor" in volumetric analysis.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Typical):**
A wastewater sample is diluted as follows: 10 mL of sample is added to 290 mL of dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is 8.0 mg/L and the DO after 5 days is 2.5 mg/L. The seed control consumed 0.5 mg/L, and the seed factor $f = 0.06$. Calculate the BOD₅ of the original sample.

**Step 1 — Compute the decimal fraction P:**

$$P = \dfrac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}} = \dfrac{10}{10 + 290} = \dfrac{10}{300} = 0.0333$$

**Step 2 — Identify the known values:**

$$D_1 = 8.0 \text{ mg/L}, \quad D_2 = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}, \quad (B_1 - B_2) = 0.5 \text{ mg/L}, \quad f = 0.06$$

**Step 3 — Apply the seeded BOD formula:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \cdot f}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(8.0 - 2.5) - (0.5)(0.06)}{0.0333}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{5.5 - 0.03}{0.0333}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{5.47}{0.0333}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 164.26 \text{ mg/L} \approx 164.3 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 4 — Validity check (KTU practical rule):**

- Depletion in sample bottle: $D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 2.5 = 5.5 \text{ mg/L}$ — **≥ 2 mg/L ✓**
- Residual DO: $D_2 = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}$ — **≥ 1 mg/L ✓**

Both conditions are satisfied, so the result is valid.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Aspect | Winkler Iodometric Method | Membrane-Electrode DO Probe |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Oxidation of Mn²⁺ → Mn⁴⁺, then titration of liberated I₂ with thiosulfate | Electrochemical reduction of O₂ at a cathode |
| Apparatus | Burette, conical flask, reagents | DO meter + probe |
| Interference | Nitrite (eliminated by azide), Fe²⁺, high organic load | H₂S, CO₂, chlorinated samples |
| Detection limit | ~0.05 mg/L | ~0.01 mg/L |
| Field suitability | Lab-based | Field-portable |
| KTU recommendation | **Standard reference method** | Permitted for routine monitoring |

### 3.4 Preparation of Dilution Water — Mass Calculations

To prepare **1 litre** of standard dilution water (APHA 5210 B):

| Reagent | Quantity per L | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Phosphate buffer (1 M, pH 7.2) | 1 mL | Maintain neutral pH |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O (22.5 g/L stock) | 1 mL | Provide Mg²⁺ for enzymes |
| CaCl₂ (27.5 g/L stock) | 1 mL | Provide Ca²⁺ for cell wall |
| FeCl₃·6H₂O (0.25 g/L stock) | 1 mL | Trace micronutrient |
| Distilled water | q.s. to 1 L | Bulk medium |
| Aeration (compressed air) | ~30 min | Saturate to ~9.1 mg/L DO at 20 °C |

The water is "aged" for **1–2 days at 20 °C** to stabilize and consume any residual organic contamination (verified by blank BOD < 0.2 mg/L).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — BOD Determination (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Sample] --> B{Sample Type?}
    B -- Domestic Sewage --> C1[Pre-dilute 1:100 or 1:1000]
    B -- Industrial Effluent --> C2[Pre-dilute + Add Seed]
    B -- Surface Water --> C3[No dilution needed]
    C1 --> D[Prepare Dilution Water with Buffer Salts]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> E[Fill BOD Bottle 300 mL]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Measure Initial DO as D1 using Winkler Method]
    F --> G[Seal with Water-Sealed Stopper]
    G --> H[Incubate at 20 deg C for 5 Days in Dark]
    H --> I[Measure Final DO as D2 after 5 Days]
    I --> J{Is D2 less than D1?}
    J -- No --> K[Invalid Test - Air Leakage]
    J -- Yes --> L[Apply BOD Formula]
    L --> M[Report BOD5 in mg/L]
    K --> N[Repeat with Correct Seal]
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 Apparatus Layout — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lab["Environmental Quality Analysis Laboratory"]
        A1[Refrigerator 4 deg C] --> A2[Sample Storage Rack]
        A3[Compressed Air Pump] --> A4[Dilution Water Tank with Aerator]
        A4 --> A5[BOD Bottles 300 mL x Multiple]
        A2 --> A5
        A5 --> A6[Incubator 20 deg C plus minus 1]
        A6 --> A7[Day 5 Sampling Station]
        A5 --> A8[Initial Sampling Station]
        A8 --> A9[Winkler Titration Setup]
        A7 --> A9
        A9 --> A10[Data Recording: D1 D2 B1 B2]
    end
    subgraph Reagents["Reagent Storage Cabinet"]
        R1[Manganese Sulfate]
        R2[Alkali Iodide Azide]
        R3[Sulfuric Acid Conc]
        R4[Sodium Thiosulfate 0.025 N]
        R5[Starch Indicator]
        R6[Phosphate Buffer pH 7.2]
    end
    Reagents --> A9
```

### 4.3 DO Sag Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pollution Discharge Point] --> Q[High BOD5 Input]
    Q --> R[Rapid DO Depletion Zone]
    R --> S[Critical DO Minimum Point of Sag]
    S --> T[Recovery Zone with Re-aeration]
    T --> U[Normal Background DO Restored]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — BOD Bottle Water Seal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Glass BOD Bottle 300 mL] --> B[Ground Glass Stopper Flared Rim]
    B --> C[Cup-Shaped Reservoir]
    C --> D[Water Seal Filled with Dilution Water]
    D --> E[Creates Air-Tight Closure]
    E --> F[Prevents Atmospheric O2 Entry]
    F --> G[Ensures Reliable 5-Day Depletion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In the KTU lab viva, you may be asked why the BOD bottle is dark-colored (amber) and stored in a dark incubator. The answer: light catalyzes **algal photosynthesis**, which would *add* oxygen to the bottle and give a falsely **low BOD** reading. The dark storage prevents this artifact.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
